LlamaFaw is a security-focused guardrail framework designed as a \\\"final layer of defense\\\" for AI agents, using ML-based classifiers to detect prompt injection, jailbreak attempts, and agent misalignment in real time. NeMoGuardrails uses a custom DSL called Colang to define conversational flows, topic boundaries, and safety rails. ClawMoak is designed to protect the host machine from the agent's actions, particularly in high-stakes environments where false negatives are costly. Best for teams running Python-based agent frameworks who need prompt injection and jailbreak detection. The only tool in this comparison is Host-level protection that monitors agent actions on the machine itself.\\n\\n## Story\\n\\nClawMoat vs LlamaFirewall vs NeMo Guardrails: Which Open-Source AI Agent Security Tool Should You Use? - DEV Community Three open-source tools. The tool includes a structured sample repository and an analytical engine built on OpenSearch to identify shared infrastructure, development patterns and behavioural similarities across large volumes of malware samples. Get started in minutes and find out more about the top techniques in our Threa\\n\\nTitle: Atomic Red Team\\nURL: https://www.atomicredteam.io/\\nText: Skip to content\\n\\nTitle: Splunking Isovalent Data: Attack Simulations and Detections\\nAuthor: Bhavin Patel, Splunk Threat Research Team\\nURL: https://www.splunk.com/en_us/blog/security/kubernetes-attack-simulations-ebpf-tetragon-splunk.html\\nText: Splunking Isovalent Data: Attack Simulations and Detections | Splunk\\n\\n# Splunking Isovalent Data: Attack Simulations and Detections\\n\\nIn [Part 1 of this series,] we focused on setting up the lab, enabling telemetry with Cisco Isovalent, via Tetragon and Hubble, and forwarding structured eBPF-derived events into Splunk Enterprise Security. With that foundation in place, we can now turn theory into action.\\n\\nIn this second part, we simulate real-world adversary behaviors inside a Kubernetes cluster to validate how Tetragon’s kernel-level visibility translates into detectable, high-fidelity security signals in Splunk. Each simulation maps to techniques in the MITRE ATT&amp;CK for Containers framework and showcases how eBPF instrumentation allows us to catch what traditional agents often miss—for example, process lineage, syscall context, and Kubernetes workload-level attribution.\\n\\nThese hands-on exercises demonstrate how Cisco Isovalent’s technology enables security teams to move from passive monitoring to proactive detection and response across Kubernetes workloads.\\n\\n## Attack Simulations and Detections\\n\\nBelow are simple kubectl-based simulations you can run inside your lab to generate realistic telemetry.After running each, inspect Splunk for process_exec, e\\n\\nTitle: Basic Usage - MITRE Caldera's documentation!\\nURL: https://caldera.readthedocs.io/en/latest/Basic-Usage.html\\nText: Basic Usage &amp;mdash; caldera documentation\\n* [] \\n* Basic Usage\\n* [View page source] \\n# Basic Usage[] \\n## Agents[] \\n### Agent Management[] \\nTo deploy an agent:\\n1. Navigate to the Agents module in the side menu under “Campaigns” and click the “Deploy an agent” button\\n2. Choose an agent (Sandcat is a good one to start with) and a platform (target operating system)\\n3. Make sure the agent options are correct (e.g. ensure&#96;app.contact.http&#96;matches the expected host and port for the Caldera server)\\n* &#96;app.contact.http&#96;represents the HTTP endpoint (including the IP/hostname and port) that the C2 server is listening on for\\nagent requests and beacons. Examples:&#96;http://localhost:8888&#96;,&#96;https://10.1.2.3&#96;,&#96;http://myc2domain.com:8080&#96;\\n* &#96;agents.implant\\\\_name&#96;represents the base name of the agent binary.\\nFor Windows agents,&#96;.exe&#96;will be automatically appended to the base name (e.g.&#96;splunkd&#96;will become&#96;splunkd.exe&#96;).\\n* &#96;agent.extensions&#96;takes in a comma-separated list of agent extensions to compile with your agent binary.\\nWhen selecting the associated deployment command, this will instruct the C2 server to compile the agent binary with the\\nrequested extensions, if they exist. If you just want a basic agent without extensions, leave this field blank.\\nSee[Sandcat extension documentation] for more information on Sandcat\\nextensions.\\n* Choose a command to execute on the targ\\n\\nTitle: Atomic Red Team™\\nAuthor: \\nURL: https://www.atomicredteam.io/atomic-red-team\\nText: Atomic Tests\\n![] \\n# Atomic Red Team™\\nAtomic Red Team is an open source library of tests that security teams can use to simulate adversarial activity in their environments.\\n[Get started] [View all atomics] \\n### Fast\\nAtomic tests run in five minutes or less and require minimal setup. Spend less time configuring and more time testing!\\n### Focused\\nSecurity teams don&amp;#x27;t want to operate with a &amp;quot;hopes and prayers&amp;quot; attitude towards detection. Atomic tests are mapped to the MITRE ATT&amp;amp;CK matrix, so you always know which techniques you do and don&amp;#x27;t detect.\\n### Community-driven\\nAtomic Red Team is open source and community developed. By working together, we can develop a fuller picture of the security landscape.\\nLoading atomic tests...\\n\\n\"}]}"
```json
}
```
Status: **completed**


</details>
</tool-use>

Today’s reality is very different. Sensitive data now lives across cloud platforms, SaaS applications, collaboration tools, browsers, and generative AI systems, while employees and contractors access and move data in constantly changing ways.\\n\\nAs a result, modern data protection strategies increasingly combine DLP, data security posture management (DSPM), and [insider risk management] (IRM):\\n\\n- IRM adds behavioral and contextual insight to understand intent and risk\\n- DSPM provides visibility into where sensitive data lives and how it is classified\\n- DLP enforces policies and prevents sensitive data from being exposed or exfiltrated\\n\\nTogether, these capabilities allow security teams to move beyond static rules and toward context-aware, risk-driven data protection.\\n\\nIn this guide, we review and compare the top DLP software solutions and vendors, evaluating how wel\\n\\nTitle: GitHub - Newer Windows10 systems typically have SMBv1 disabled. For this reason ... - SMTP - The client creates a mail message placing the data in the body of the message or if a file, as an attachment. As noted above, determine which system you are egressing from and their security policies to see if you can use the old ... - DNS_TXT - Data\\n\\n## GitHub - cyberisltd/Egresser: Client/server scripts designed to test outbound (egress) firewall rules.\\nhttps://github.com/cyberisltd/Egresser\\n\\n|\\n|\\n README.md \\n|\\n README.md \\n|\\n|\\n| egresser-server.pl \\n|\\n egresser-server.pl \\n|\\n|\\n|\\n egresser.pl \\n|\\n egresser.pl ... # Egresser\\n \\n* Author: geoff.jones@cyberis.co.uk \\n* Copyright: Cyberis Limited 2013 * License: GPLv3 (See LICENSE)\\nClient/server scripts designed to test outbound (egress) firewall rules.\\n## Description Egresser is a tool to enumerate outbound firewall rules, designed for penetration testers to assess whether egress filtering is adequate from within a corporate network. Probing each TCP port in turn, the Egresser server will respond with the client’s source IP address and port, allowing the client to determine whether or not the outbound port is ... The client is also written in Perl and is threaded for speed. By default it will scan TCP ports 1-1024, although this is configurable within the script. It is possible to force IPv4 with the ‘-4’ command line argument, or IPv6 with ‘-6’; ... *Why?* It is recommended that outbound firewall rules are restricted within corporate environments to ensure perimeter ... Claude calls three APIs: SSL certificate check, security header analysis, email authentication (SPF, DKIM, DMARC), and email list\\n\\n## How I Built a Deterministic Multi-Agent Dev Pipeline Inside OpenClaw (and Contributed a Missing Piece to Lobster)\\nhttps://dev.to/ggondim/how-i-built-a-deterministic-multi-agent-dev-pipeline-inside-openclaw-and-contributed-a-missing-4ool\\n\\n# How I Built a Deterministic Multi-Agent Dev Pipeline Inside OpenClaw (and Contributed a Missing Piece to Lobster) - DEV Community\\n\\nPublished: 2026-02-23T01:40:51+00:00\\nType: Non-news\\n\\n## Summary\\n\\nThe author has spent two months building a Deterministic Multi-Agent Dev Pipeline inside OpenClaw, a platform that allows autonomous AI agents to work autonomously in a GitHub Actions environment. <think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Recommending targeted DLP test tools**</details></think>

---

Yes — and your approach is the right one: **don’t block all egress**, detect/block **exfil behavior patterns**.

Best open-source tools for your lab:

- **egress0r** (most DLP-focused)  
  Purpose-built to test DLP/firewall controls across multiple channels (FTP/DNS/SMTP/TCP/UDP/HTTP-like services).  
  URL: https://github.com/cyllective/egress0r  
  Service/docs: https://egress0r.io/

- **Egress-Assess**  
  Classic exfil simulation framework (FTP/HTTP/HTTPS/DNS/SMB/SMTP) for testing whether data escapes and gets detected.  
  URL: https://github.com/RedSiege/Egress-Assess

- **exfilkit**  
  Lightweight data exfiltration utility for detection testing.  
  URL: https://github.com/tasooshi/exfilkit

- **Atomic Red Team** (great for repeatable ATT&CK tests)  
  Not DLP-only, but very useful to simulate known exfil techniques and validate detections.  
  URL: https://github.com/redcanaryco/atomic-red-team  
  Example exfil technique test: https://github.com/redcanaryco/atomic-red-team/blob/master/atomics/T1041/T1041.md  
  MITRE reference: https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1041/

If you want, I can continue from the previous implementation and adjust your `coder-secure` scenario to:
1) keep normal outbound traffic allowed,  
2) run `egress0r`/Atomic exfil tests with synthetic sensitive patterns,  
3) enforce only Tetragon policies that match toxic behavior (large read + encode/compress + outbound transfer chain).

---

_**User**_

ok Atomic Red Team, MITRE Caldera or Stratus Red Team seems fine tool to try here (the others are less popular/small community traction).
First let's try a simple pattern.

I’ll inspect the current Tetragon policy files and wire the smallest safe rule first.

```json
      "serverName": "context7"
    }
  ]
}
```

```json

  "status": "success"
}
```
Result:

```json
{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### Applying Namespaced Tetragon TracingPolicy\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/k8s-filtering.md\\n\\nApplies a TracingPolicyNamespaced resource to the 'default' Kubernetes namespace. This policy is configured to trigger a Sigkill action when the sys_lseek system call is invoked with the first argument (file descriptor) equal to -1.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;Shell\\ncat &lt;&lt; EOF | kubectl apply -n default -f -\\napiVersion: cilium.io/v1alpha1\\nkind: TracingPolicyNamespaced\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"lseek-namespaced\\\"\\nspec:\\n  kprobes:\\n  - call: \\\"sys_lseek\\\"\\n    syscall: true\\n    args:\\n    - index: 0\\n      type: \\\"int\\\"\\n    selectors:\\n    - matchArgs:\\n      - index: 0\\n        operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n        values:\\n        - \\\"-1\\\"\\n      matchActions:\\n      - action: Sigkill\\nEOF\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Define Tracing Policy Selectors and Actions (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/example.md\\n\\nSpecifies selectors to filter events based on argument values and defines the action to take for matching events. This selector matches when the file argument (index 1) is exactly &#96;/tmp/tetragon&#96; and triggers a &#96;SIGKILL&#96;.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\n    selectors:\\n    - matchArgs:\\n      - index: 1\\n        operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n        values:\\n        - \\\"/tmp/tetragon\\\"\\n      matchActions:\\n      - action: Sigkill\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Basic matchBinaries Selector (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nDefines a basic &#96;matchBinaries&#96; selector using the &#96;In&#96; operator to match a specific binary path (&#96;/usr/sbin/sshd&#96;). This selector is used to identify processes based on their executable path.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;YAML\\n- matchBinaries:\\n  - operator: \\\"In\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"/usr/sbin/sshd\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Filter by Specific Binary Paths (matchBinaries, In) - YAML\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis selector filters events based on the path of the executable binary. It uses the 'In' operator to match events originating from processes executing '/usr/bin/cat' or '/usr/bin/tail'. By default, 'followForks' is true.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;YAML\\n- matchBinaries:\\n  - operator: \\\"In\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"/usr/bin/cat\\\"\\n    - \\\"/usr/bin/tail\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Filter by Specific File Paths (matchArgs, Equal) - YAML\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis selector configuration filters events based on the value of a function argument at index 1. It uses the 'Equal' operator to match against the exact paths '/etc/passwd' or '/etc/shadow'. This is typically used for arguments representing file paths.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;YAML\\nselectors:\\n- matchArgs:\\n  - index: 1\\n    operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"/etc/passwd\\\"\\n    - \\\"/etc/shadow\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\"}]}"
}
```

```json
{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### Apply Tetragon TCP Connect TracingPolicy (Bash)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/use-cases/network-observability.md\\n\\nApplies the example TCP connect TracingPolicy from the Cilium Tetragon GitHub repository. This configures Tetragon to monitor TCP connect events using kprobe hooks.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\\nkubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cilium/tetragon/main/examples/tracingpolicy/tcp-connect.yaml\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Filter tcp_connect events with jq (Shell)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/policy-library/observability/_index.md\\n\\nDemonstrates using &#96;jq&#96; to filter Tetragon &#96;process_kprobe&#96; events, specifically targeting the &#96;tcp_connect&#96; function. It extracts and formats key information including time, process details, and source/destination socket addresses and ports. This is used for monitoring outbound connections.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;Shell\\njq 'select(.process_kprobe != null) | select(.process_kprobe.function_name | test(\\\"tcp_connect\\\")) | \\\"\\\\(.time) \\\\(.process_kprobe.process.binary) \\\\(.process_kprobe.process.arguments) \\\\(.process_kprobe.args[0].sock_arg.saddr):\\\\(.process_kprobe.args[0].sock_arg.sport) -&gt; \\\\(.process_kprobe.args[0].sock_arg.daddr):\\\\(.process_kprobe.args[0].sock_arg.dport)\\\"'\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Monitor sys_write for SSH and Children (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nDefines a Tetragon &#96;kprobe&#96; policy to monitor the &#96;sys_write&#96; syscall. It uses &#96;matchBinaries&#96; to target &#96;/usr/sbin/sshd&#96; (and potentially its children if &#96;followChildren&#96; is enabled elsewhere) and &#96;matchArgs&#96; to filter writes to file descriptors 1, 2, or 3 (stdout, stderr, stdin).\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;YAML\\n- call: \\\"sys_write\\\"\\n  syscall: true\\n  args:\\n  - index: 0\\n    type: \\\"int\\\"\\n  - index: 1\\n    type: \\\"char_buf\\\"\\n    sizeArgIndex: 3\\n  - index: 2\\n    type: \\\"size_t\\\"\\n  selectors:\\n  # match to /sbin/sshd\\n  - matchBinaries:\\n    - operator: \\\"In\\\"\\n      values:\\n      - \\\"/usr/sbin/sshd\\\"\\n  # match to stdin/stdout/stderr\\n    matchArgs:\\n    - index: 0\\n      operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n      values:\\n      - \\\"1\\\"\\n      - \\\"2\\\"\\n      - \\\"3\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Basic matchBinaries Selector (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nDefines a basic &#96;matchBinaries&#96; selector using the &#96;In&#96; operator to match a specific binary path (&#96;/usr/sbin/sshd&#96;). This selector is used to identify processes based on their executable path.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;YAML\\n- matchBinaries:\\n  - operator: \\\"In\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"/usr/sbin/sshd\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Filter by Specific Binary Paths (matchBinaries, In) - YAML\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis selector filters events based on the path of the executable binary. It uses the 'In' operator to match events originating from processes executing '/usr/bin/cat' or '/usr/bin/tail'. By default, 'followForks' is true.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;YAML\\n- matchBinaries:\\n  - operator: \\\"In\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"/usr/bin/cat\\\"\\n    - \\\"/usr/bin/tail\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\"}]}"
}
```

```json
{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### Matching Argument with Equal Operator (Single Value) - YAML\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis YAML snippet demonstrates how to use the &#96;Equal&#96; operator within &#96;matchArgs&#96; to match a specific argument at index 0 if its value is exactly \\\"/etc/passwd\\\".\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\n      matchArgs:\\n      - index: 0\\n        operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n        values:\\n        - \\\"/etc/passwd\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Filter by Specific File Paths (matchArgs, Equal) - YAML\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis selector configuration filters events based on the value of a function argument at index 1. It uses the 'Equal' operator to match against the exact paths '/etc/passwd' or '/etc/shadow'. This is typically used for arguments representing file paths.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;YAML\\nselectors:\\n- matchArgs:\\n  - index: 1\\n    operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"/etc/passwd\\\"\\n    - \\\"/etc/shadow\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Filter by File Descriptor and Path Prefix (matchArgs, Equal/Prefix) - YAML\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis selector combines two argument filters. It filters events where the argument at index 0 (often a file descriptor) is exactly '3' AND the argument at index 1 (often a file path) starts with '/etc'. This allows for more specific filtering based on multiple argument properties.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;YAML\\n- matchArgs:\\n  - index: 0\\n    operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"3\\\"\\n  - index: 1\\n    operator: \\\"Prefix\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"/etc\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Matching Argument with Equal Operator (Multiple Values) - YAML\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis YAML snippet shows the &#96;Equal&#96; operator with multiple values. It matches the argument at index 0 if its value is any of the listed values (\\\"arg0\\\", \\\"arg1\\\", \\\"arg2\\\"). This behaves as an OR operation.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\nmatchArgs:\\n- index: 0\\n  operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n  values:\\n  - \\\"arg0\\\"\\n  - \\\"arg1\\\"\\n  - \\\"arg2\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### YAML: Filtering syscalls by PIDs (NotIn) and multiple arg values\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis YAML configuration demonstrates filtering syscalls based on PIDs not in a specific set (0, 1, following forks) and where the first argument (index 0) is equal to either '/etc/passwd' or '/etc/shadow'. Multiple values within 'matchArgs' use OR logic, while 'matchPIDs' and 'matchArgs' are combined with AND logic.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\nselectors:\\n - matchPIDs:\\n   - operator: NotIn\\n     followForks: true\\n     values:\\n     - 0\\n     - 1\\n  matchArgs:\\n  - index: 0\\n    operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"/etc/passwd\\\"\\n    - \\\"/etc/shadow\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\"}]}"
}
```
Status: **completed**


</details>
</tool-use>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Planning deployment policy revisions**</details></think>

---

I’m implementing this as a first incremental rule: **only kill external connections initiated by known exfil binaries** (`curl`/`wget`/`nc`) in `coder-secure`, and remove the blanket egress deny from enforcement. Then we can layer a “large file read + exfil” correlation rule next.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Updating tasks to delete existing resource**</details></think>

---

I found one important detail: selector blocks are OR’ed if split, so I merged binary+destination checks into the same selector to avoid accidental blanket blocking. I’m now updating the Ansible role to stop applying the deny-all Cilium egress policy and explicitly remove it if present.

---

I’ve finished the code changes for the simple pattern. Next I’ll apply just the updated secure-namespace policies directly in-cluster (no full redeploy) and verify the active resources.

---

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: kubectl apply -n coder-secure -f roles/tetragon/files/policy-enforce-egress-block.yml && kubectl apply -n coder-secure -f roles/tetragon/files/policy-enforce-sensitive-read.yml && kubectl delete ciliumnetworkpolicy deny-external-egress -n coder-secure --ignore-not-found && kubectl -n coder-secure get tracingpoliciesnamespaced && kubectl -n coder-secure get ciliumnetworkpolicies</summary>

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: mise x -- coder ssh --help</summary>

```bash
mise x -- coder ssh --help
```

```
coder v2.30.2+fa050ee

USAGE:
  coder ssh [flags] &lt;workspace&gt; [command]

  Start a shell into a workspace or run a command

  This command does not have full parity with the standard SSH command. For
  users who need the full functionality of SSH, create an ssh configuration with
  &#96;coder config-ssh&#96;.
  
    - Use &#96;--&#96; to separate and pass flags directly to the command executed via
  SSH.:
  
       $ coder ssh &lt;workspace&gt; -- ls -la

OPTIONS:
      --disable-autostart bool, $CODER_SSH_DISABLE_AUTOSTART (default: false)
          Disable starting the workspace automatically when connecting via SSH.

  -e, --env string-array, $CODER_SSH_ENV
          Set environment variable(s) for session (key1=value1,key2=value2,...).

  -A, --forward-agent bool, $CODER_SSH_FORWARD_AGENT
          Specifies whether to forward the SSH agent specified in
          $SSH_AUTH_SOCK.

  -G, --forward-gpg bool, $CODER_SSH_FORWARD_GPG
          Specifies whether to forward the GPG agent. Unsupported on Windows
          workspaces, but supports all clients. Requires gnupg (gpg, gpgconf) on
          both the client and workspace. The GPG agent must already be running
          locally and will not be started for you. If a GPG agent is already
          running in the workspace, it will be attempted to be killed.

      --hostname-suffix string, $CODER_SSH_HOSTNAME_SUFFIX
          Strip this suffix from the provided hostname to determine the
          workspace name. This is useful when used as part of an OpenSSH proxy
          command. The suffix must be specified without a leading . character.

      --identity-agent string, $CODER_SSH_IDENTITY_AGENT
          Specifies which identity agent to use (overrides $SSH_AUTH_SOCK),
          forward agent must also be enabled.

  -l, --log-dir string, $CODER_SSH_LOG_DIR
          Specify the directory containing SSH diagnostic log files.

      --network-info-dir string
          Specifies a directory to write network information periodically.

      --network-info-interval duration (default: 5s)
          Specifies the interval to update network information.

      --no-wait bool, $CODER_SSH_NO_WAIT
          Enter workspace immediately after the agent has connected. This is the
          default if the template has configured the agent startup script
          behavior as non-blocking.
          DEPRECATED: Use --wait instead.

  -R, --remote-forward string-array, $CODER_SSH_REMOTE_FORWARD
          Enable remote port forwarding (remote_port:local_address:local_port).

      --ssh-host-prefix string, $CODER_SSH_SSH_HOST_PREFIX
          Strip this prefix from the provided hostname to determine the
          workspace name. This is useful when used as part of an OpenSSH proxy
          command.

      --stdio bool, $CODER_SSH_STDIO
          Specifies whether to emit SSH output over stdin/stdout.

      --wait yes|no|auto, $CODER_SSH_WAIT (default: auto)
          Specifies whether or not to wait for the startup script to finish
          executing. Auto means that the agent startup script behavior
          configured in the workspace template is used.

      --workspace-poll-interval duration, $CODER_WORKSPACE_POLL_INTERVAL (default: 1m)
          Specifies how often to poll for workspace automated shutdown.

———
Run &#96;coder --help&#96; for a list of global options.

```
</details>
</tool-use>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Summarizing final actions and next steps**</details></think>

---

Implemented and tested a **first simple pattern** in `coder-secure`: block likely exfil tooling to external destinations, without blanket network deny.

### What I changed

- Updated `roles/tetragon/files/policy-enforce-egress-block.yml`
  - Kept `tcp_connect` enforcement.
  - Added `matchBinaries` filter for:
    - `/usr/bin/curl`
    - `/usr/bin/wget`
    - `/usr/bin/nc`
  - Kept `NotDAddr` for external destinations.
  - Result: only these tools are killed on external connect attempts.
- Updated `roles/tetragon/tasks/main.yml`
  - Stopped applying `cilium-netpol-deny-egress.yml`.
  - Added deletion of legacy `deny-external-egress` CiliumNetworkPolicy.
- Updated `templates/podman-workspace-secure/main.tf`
  - Startup message now says selective controls (not full egress block).
- Updated `scripts/demo-dataleakage.sh`
  - Removed the old “block all external traffic” assumption.
  - Fixed internal Coder service test port from `3000` to `80`.

### Applied to cluster

- Applied updated TracingPolicies in `coder-secure`.
- Deleted old deny-all CiliumNetworkPolicy.
- Verified:
  - `TracingPolicyNamespaced`: present and configured.
  - `CiliumNetworkPolicy`: none remaining in `coder-secure`.

### Live validation done

I created a secure workspace: `dlp-simple` (template `podman-workspace-secure`) and tested:

- `curl https://example.com` → **killed** (exit 137) ✅
- `wget https://example.com` → **killed** (exit 137) ✅
- `cat /etc/shadow` → **killed** (exit 137) ✅
- `python -c "urllib.request.urlopen('https://example.com')"` → **200** ✅ (shows non-targeted egress still allowed)
- `curl http://coder.coder.svc.cluster.local:80` works manually from workspace ✅

If you want, next step is a more behavior-specific rule:
- **large file read** (`open/read` on path + size threshold approximation) **then external exfil command** (`curl -T`, `curl --data-binary`, `wget --post-file`) to tighten this beyond binary-only matching.

---

_**User**_

yes let's go

---

_**Agent (model gpt-5.3-codex, mode Agent)**_

Great, next step will be to move from binary-only blocking to a **toxic command pattern**: block upload-style `curl`/`wget` arguments (data/file upload), while allowing normal outbound requests. I’ll pull the exact Tetragon syntax from Context7, then patch the policy and demo script accordingly.

<tool-use data-tool-type="unknown" data-tool-name="mcp-context7-user-context7-query-docs">
<details>
<summary>Tool use: **mcp-context7-user-context7-query-docs**</summary>


Parameters:

```json
{
  "tools": [
    {
      "name": "query-docs",
      "parameters": "{\"libraryId\":\"/cilium/tetragon\",\"query\":\"examples of enforcing on execve command arguments with matchArgs index for sys_execve or sys_execveat and matchBinaries, using Prefix/Equal/Postfix operators and Sigkill action\"}",
      "serverName": "context7"
    }
  ]
}
```
Additional data:

```json
{
  "status": "success"
}
```
Result:

```json
{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### Define Tracing Policy Selectors and Actions (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/example.md\\n\\nSpecifies selectors to filter events based on argument values and defines the action to take for matching events. This selector matches when the file argument (index 1) is exactly &#96;/tmp/tetragon&#96; and triggers a &#96;SIGKILL&#96;.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\n    selectors:\\n    - matchArgs:\\n      - index: 1\\n        operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n        values:\\n        - \\\"/tmp/tetragon\\\"\\n      matchActions:\\n      - action: Sigkill\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Define Tracing Policy for fd_install (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/example.md\\n\\nDefines a complete Cilium Tetragon TracingPolicy resource. It hooks the &#96;fd_install&#96; kernel function, filters events where the file argument (index 1) is &#96;/tmp/tetragon&#96;, and triggers a &#96;Sigkill&#96; action on matching processes.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\napiVersion: cilium.io/v1alpha1\\nkind: TracingPolicy\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"fd-install\\\"\\nspec:\\n  kprobes:\\n  - call: \\\"fd_install\\\"\\n    syscall: false\\n    args:\\n    - index: 0\\n      type: \\\"int\\\"\\n    - index: 1\\n      type: \\\"file\\\"\\n    selectors:\\n    - matchArgs:\\n      - index: 1\\n        operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n        values:\\n        - \\\"/tmp/tetragon\\\"\\n      matchActions:\\n      - action: Sigkill\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Send Signal Action (Tetragon YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis YAML snippet illustrates the use of the &#96;Signal&#96; action in a Tetragon TracingPolicy. It sends a specified signal (SIGKILL, signal 9, in this example) to the process making a &#96;sys_write&#96; call to &#96;/etc/passwd&#96; if its PID is not 0 or 1, achieving a similar effect to the &#96;Sigkill&#96; action.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\n- call: \\\"sys_write\\\"\\n  syscall: true\\n  args:\\n  - index: 0\\n    type: \\\"fd\\\"\\n  - index: 1\\n    type: \\\"char_buf\\\"\\n    sizeArgIndex: 3\\n  - index: 2\\n    type: \\\"size_t\\\"\\n  selectors:\\n  - matchPIDs:\\n    - operator: NotIn\\n      followForks: true\\n      isNamespacePID: true\\n      values:\\n      - 0\\n      - 1\\n    matchArgs:\\n    - index: 0\\n      operator: \\\"Prefix\\\"\\n      values:\\n      - \\\"/etc/passwd\\\"\\n    matchActions:\\n    - action: Signal\\n      argSig: 9\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Kprobe-Multi Sigkill Alternative Example (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nShows an alternative policy using &#96;kprobe_multi&#96; support for newer kernels, achieving the same outcome as the NotifyEnforcer example (killing bash on dup/dup2). It uses a &#96;kprobes&#96; definition with a syscall list and the &#96;Sigkill&#96; action.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\nspec:\\n  lists:\\n  - name: \\\"syscalls\\\"\\n    type: \\\"syscalls\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"sys_dup\\\"\\n    - \\\"sys_dup2\\\"\\n  kprobes:\\n  - call: \\\"list:syscalls\\\"\\n    selectors:\\n    - matchBinaries:\\n      - operator: \\\"In\\\"\\n        values:\\n      - \\\"/usr/bin/bash\\\"\\n      matchActions:\\n      - action: \\\"Sigkill\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Matching Argument with Equal Operator (Single Value) - YAML\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis YAML snippet demonstrates how to use the &#96;Equal&#96; operator within &#96;matchArgs&#96; to match a specific argument at index 0 if its value is exactly \\\"/etc/passwd\\\".\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\n      matchArgs:\\n      - index: 0\\n        operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n        values:\\n        - \\\"/etc/passwd\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\"}]}"
}
```
Status: **completed**


</details>
</tool-use>

```json
      "serverName": "context7"
    }
  ]
}
```
Additional data:

```json
{
  "status": "success"
}
```
Result:

```json
{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### Configuring Multiple Selectors with OR Logic in Tetragon (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis YAML configuration demonstrates how to define multiple selectors in Tetragon. When multiple selectors are present, they are combined using a logical OR operation. Each selector can include criteria like matching PIDs and specific argument values.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\nselectors:\\n - matchPIDs:\\n   - operator: In\\n     followForks: true\\n     values:\\n     - pid1\\n     - pid2\\n     - pid3\\n  matchArgs:\\n  - index: 0\\n    operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - 1\\n  - index: 2\\n    operator: \\\"lt\\\"\\n    values:\\n    -  500\\n - matchPIDs:\\n   - operator: In\\n     followForks: true\\n     values:\\n     - pid1\\n     - pid2\\n     - pid3\\n  matchArgs:\\n  - index: 0\\n    operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - 2\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Defining Arguments While Skipping Others\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/hooks.md\\n\\nShows how to define only specific arguments you are interested in, omitting others like the first argument (index 0) in this example. This tells the Tetragon printer to skip the omitted arguments in the output.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\nargs:\\n- index: 1\\n  type: \\\"char_buf\\\"\\n  returnCopy: true\\n  sizeArgIndex: 3\\n- index: 2\\n  type: \\\"size_t\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Example Tetragon TracingPolicy for Raw Syscalls (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/hooks.md\\n\\nProvides a YAML definition for a Tetragon &#96;TracingPolicy&#96; that configures tracepoints to observe all raw syscall entries (&#96;raw_syscalls:sys_enter&#96;) and specifically extracts the syscall ID from the 4th argument.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;YAML\\napiVersion: cilium.io/v1alpha1\\nkind: TracingPolicy\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"raw-syscalls\\\"\\nspec:\\n  tracepoints:\\n  - subsystem: \\\"raw_syscalls\\\"\\n    event: \\\"sys_enter\\\"\\n    args:\\n    - index: 4\\n      type: \\\"int64\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Filter by File Descriptor and Path Prefix (matchArgs, Equal/Prefix) - YAML\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis selector combines two argument filters. It filters events where the argument at index 0 (often a file descriptor) is exactly '3' AND the argument at index 1 (often a file path) starts with '/etc'. This allows for more specific filtering based on multiple argument properties.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;YAML\\n- matchArgs:\\n  - index: 0\\n    operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"3\\\"\\n  - index: 1\\n    operator: \\\"Prefix\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"/etc\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### YAML: Filtering syscalls by PIDs (NotIn) and multiple arg values\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nThis YAML configuration demonstrates filtering syscalls based on PIDs not in a specific set (0, 1, following forks) and where the first argument (index 0) is equal to either '/etc/passwd' or '/etc/shadow'. Multiple values within 'matchArgs' use OR logic, while 'matchPIDs' and 'matchArgs' are combined with AND logic.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\nselectors:\\n - matchPIDs:\\n   - operator: NotIn\\n     followForks: true\\n     values:\\n     - 0\\n     - 1\\n  matchArgs:\\n  - index: 0\\n    operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n    values:\\n    - \\\"/etc/passwd\\\"\\n    - \\\"/etc/shadow\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\"}]}"
}
```
Status: **completed**


</details>
</tool-use>

```json
{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### Define Tracing Policy for fd_install (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/example.md\\n\\nDefines a complete Cilium Tetragon TracingPolicy resource. It hooks the &#96;fd_install&#96; kernel function, filters events where the file argument (index 1) is &#96;/tmp/tetragon&#96;, and triggers a &#96;Sigkill&#96; action on matching processes.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\napiVersion: cilium.io/v1alpha1\\nkind: TracingPolicy\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"fd-install\\\"\\nspec:\\n  kprobes:\\n  - call: \\\"fd_install\\\"\\n    syscall: false\\n    args:\\n    - index: 0\\n      type: \\\"int\\\"\\n    - index: 1\\n      type: \\\"file\\\"\\n    selectors:\\n    - matchArgs:\\n      - index: 1\\n        operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n        values:\\n        - \\\"/tmp/tetragon\\\"\\n      matchActions:\\n      - action: Sigkill\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Define Tracing Policy Selectors and Actions (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/example.md\\n\\nSpecifies selectors to filter events based on argument values and defines the action to take for matching events. This selector matches when the file argument (index 1) is exactly &#96;/tmp/tetragon&#96; and triggers a &#96;SIGKILL&#96;.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\n    selectors:\\n    - matchArgs:\\n      - index: 1\\n        operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n        values:\\n        - \\\"/tmp/tetragon\\\"\\n      matchActions:\\n      - action: Sigkill\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Defining char_buf Argument with Advanced Options\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/hooks.md\\n\\nExample demonstrating the definition of a 'char_buf' argument, including 'returnCopy' to read data at kretprobe and 'sizeArgIndex' to specify the index of the argument containing the buffer size. This is useful for functions like 'sys_write'.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\n- call: \\\"sys_write\\\"\\n  syscall: true\\n  args:\\n  - index: 0\\n    type: \\\"int\\\"\\n  - index: 1\\n    type: \\\"char_buf\\\"\\n    returnCopy: true\\n    sizeArgIndex: 3\\n  - index: 2\\n    type: \\\"size_t\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Monitor sys_write for SSH and Children (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/selectors.md\\n\\nDefines a Tetragon &#96;kprobe&#96; policy to monitor the &#96;sys_write&#96; syscall. It uses &#96;matchBinaries&#96; to target &#96;/usr/sbin/sshd&#96; (and potentially its children if &#96;followChildren&#96; is enabled elsewhere) and &#96;matchArgs&#96; to filter writes to file descriptors 1, 2, or 3 (stdout, stderr, stdin).\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;YAML\\n- call: \\\"sys_write\\\"\\n  syscall: true\\n  args:\\n  - index: 0\\n    type: \\\"int\\\"\\n  - index: 1\\n    type: \\\"char_buf\\\"\\n    sizeArgIndex: 3\\n  - index: 2\\n    type: \\\"size_t\\\"\\n  selectors:\\n  # match to /sbin/sshd\\n  - matchBinaries:\\n    - operator: \\\"In\\\"\\n      values:\\n      - \\\"/usr/sbin/sshd\\\"\\n  # match to stdin/stdout/stderr\\n    matchArgs:\\n    - index: 0\\n      operator: \\\"Equal\\\"\\n      values:\\n      - \\\"1\\\"\\n      - \\\"2\\\"\\n      - \\\"3\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Accessing Raw Tracepoint Argument (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/cilium/tetragon/blob/main/docs/content/en/docs/concepts/tracing-policy/hooks.md\\n\\nShows how to configure a Tetragon policy to access a specific argument of a raw tracepoint by specifying its index and type in the 'args' list.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;YAML\\napiversion: cilium.io/v1alpha1\\nkind: tracingpolicy\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"rawtp\\\"\\nspec:\\n  tracepoints:\\n    - subsystem: \\\"sched\\\"\\n      event: \\\"sched_process_exec\\\"\\n      raw: true\\n      args:\\n        - index: 2\\n          type: \\\"linux_binprm\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\"}]}"
}
```
Status: **completed**


</details>
</tool-use>

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: set -euo pipefail; echo 'TEST A curl external allowed'; if mise x -- coder ssh dlp-simple 'sh -lc "curl --max-time 5 -s https://example.com >/dev/null"'; then echo 'PASS'; else echo 'FAIL'; fi; echo 'TEST B wget external allowed'; if mise x -- coder ssh dlp-simple 'sh -lc "wget --timeout=5 -q -O /dev/null https://example.com"'; then echo 'PASS'; else echo 'FAIL'; fi; echo 'TEST C python external allowed'; if mise x -- coder ssh dlp-simple 'python -c "import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlopen(\"https://example.com\", timeout=5)"'; then echo 'PASS'; else echo 'FAIL'; fi; echo 'TEST D prepare dlp file allowed'; if mise x -- coder ssh dlp-simple 'sh -lc "mkdir -p /tmp/dlp-sensitive && dd if=/dev/zero of=/tmp/dlp-sensitive/payload.bin bs=1M count=5 status=none"'; then echo 'PASS'; else echo 'FAIL'; fi; echo 'TEST E base64 dlp file blocked'; if mise x -- coder ssh dlp-simple 'sh -lc "base64 /tmp/dlp-sensitive/payload.bin >/dev/null"'; then echo 'FAIL'; else echo 'PASS'; fi; echo 'TEST F nc outbound blocked'; if mise x -- coder ssh dlp-simple 'sh -lc "echo test | nc -w 3 93.184.216.34 80 >/dev/null"'; then echo 'FAIL'; else echo 'PASS'; fi</summary>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Summarizing implementation and next steps**</details></think>

---

Implemented. We now have a **simple toxic-pattern model** instead of broad egress blocking.

### What changed

- Added a new enforcement policy: `roles/tetragon/files/policy-enforce-dlp-zone-read.yml`
  - Kills process when these binaries open files under `/tmp/dlp-sensitive`:
    - `base64`
    - `tar`
    - `gzip`
- Kept `roles/tetragon/files/policy-enforce-egress-block.yml` narrow (only `nc` external connect check).
- Updated Ansible role to deploy the new policy:
  - `roles/tetragon/tasks/main.yml`
- Updated demo scenario:
  - `scripts/demo-dataleakage.sh`
  - Now demonstrates:
    1) benign external traffic allowed (`curl`/`wget`/`python`)
    2) toxic data access from protected zone blocked (`base64 /tmp/dlp-sensitive/...`)
    3) sensitive system reads still blocked (`/etc/shadow`, etc.)
    4) internal service traffic allowed

### Applied and validated live

In workspace `dlp-simple`:

- External benign traffic:
  - `curl` ✅ allowed
  - `wget` ✅ allowed
  - `python urllib` ✅ allowed
- Toxic pattern:
  - `base64 /tmp/dlp-sensitive/payload.bin` ✅ blocked (killed / exit 137)
- No blanket egress deny:
  - raw bash `/dev/tcp` test ✅ allowed

### Next step (if you want)

I can now add an **Atomic Red Team-style test wrapper** in the workspace that:
- generates synthetic “sensitive” files,
- runs a fixed sequence of exfil-like commands,
- asserts expected allow/block outcomes automatically.

---

_**User**_

can a policy still allow small file push but no massive data ?

I always hear about gigabytes or terabytes of data beeing exfiltrated from companies. How could some much traffic not be shortcut, let alone not be detected? 

---

_**Agent (model gpt-5.3-codex, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Explaining multi-layer exfiltration detection**</details></think>

---

Yes — and that’s exactly how mature controls are usually designed: **allow normal small transfers, block or throttle bulk exfiltration**.

### Short answer

- **Yes**, you can allow small file pushes and stop large-volume exfil.
- But it usually needs **multiple control layers**, not one single Tetragon rule.

### In your current setup (important nuance)

With Tetragon alone, you’re very good at:
- detecting/blocking **process + behavior patterns** (who executed what),
- killing suspicious flows quickly.

Tetragon is **not ideal as a standalone byte-metering DLP engine** (e.g., “allow up to 20 MB/day/user then block”).  
For that, add a network enforcement/telemetry layer.

### Practical pattern that works well

- **Layer 1: Behavior guard (Tetragon)**
  - Block suspicious chains (`read sensitive path` + `encode/compress` + `egress tool`).
- **Layer 2: Network guard (Cilium/egress gateway/proxy)**
  - Enforce destination allowlists.
  - Add bandwidth/connection-rate limits.
  - Force outbound through inspected proxy.
- **Layer 3: Detection analytics (Hubble/NetFlow/SIEM)**
  - Alert on unusual volume, bursty uploads, off-hours transfer, rare destinations.

So yes: *small push allowed, massive push denied* is very doable when you combine these.

---

On your second question (“how can GB/TB leave undetected?”): it usually happens because of gaps like:

- No strict egress filtering (too many open destinations).
- No baseline of “normal” transfer volume per host/user/app.
- Encrypted HTTPS outbound with no proxy visibility.
- Alerts exist but are too noisy / unactioned.
- Exfil spread slowly over time (“low and slow”) to evade thresholds.
- Data moved by trusted tools/accounts, so it looked legitimate.

If you want, next I can propose a concrete policy stack for your lab:
- exact thresholds (example: 50 MB per transfer, 500 MB/hour),
- what to enforce in Cilium vs Tetragon,
- and a demo script proving “5 MB allowed, 500 MB blocked/alerted.”

---

_**User**_

yes

---

_**Agent (model gpt-5.3-codex, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Proposing detailed policy stack with demo**</details></think>

---

Perfect — here’s a concrete stack you can use in your current lab (`Tetragon + Cilium + Coder`) to get **small allowed / massive blocked or alerted**.

## Target Policy

- **Allow:** uploads up to `10 MB` to approved destinations.
- **Alert:** cumulative egress over `200 MB/hour` per workspace.
- **Block:** single upload over `50 MB` (except explicit allowlisted service accounts).
- **Block immediately:** sensitive-path + encode/compress + outbound tool patterns.

## Control Split (who does what)

- **Tetragon (behavior):**
  - Keep your current “toxic pattern” enforcement.
  - Add/keep rules for:
    - sensitive path read by `base64|gzip|tar`
    - suspicious exfil tooling invocation
- **Cilium (network path):**
  - Force workspace egress through one controlled egress point (proxy/gateway).
  - Allow only DNS + proxy destination (no direct internet).
- **Proxy (size enforcement):**
  - Enforce `client_max_body_size`/request-body limit (`50 MB`).
  - Return hard failure (`413`) when payload exceeds limit.
- **Observability (volume detection):**
  - Hubble/flow logs -> metrics/alerts for per-pod bytes over threshold (`200 MB/hour`).

## Why this catches “GB/TB exfil”

Large incidents usually slip through when only one layer exists.  
This design catches both:
- **fast bulk transfer** (proxy hard limit),
- **slow drip exfil** (hourly cumulative byte alerts).

## Demo Scenario (simple and realistic)

- **Test A (allowed):** upload `5 MB` file -> success.
- **Test B (blocked):** upload `100 MB` file -> proxy rejects (`413`).
- **Test C (alert):** send many `8 MB` chunks for >`200 MB/hour` -> alert fires.
- **Test D (behavior block):** `base64 /tmp/dlp-sensitive/... | curl ...` -> killed by Tetragon.

---

If you want, next step I can implement this in your repo in two increments:

1) **Now:** add egress choke point + proxy body-size limit + demo script (`5MB pass / 100MB fail`).  
2) **Then:** add cumulative-byte alert rule (`200MB/hour per workspace`) wired to your existing telemetry.

```json
{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### Allow Egress to kube-dns in kube-system Namespace\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/security/policy/kubernetes\\n\\nThis example allows all pods in the 'public' namespace to communicate with kube-dns on port 53/UDP in the 'kube-system' namespace. It uses CiliumNetworkPolicy and specifies egress rules with endpoint and port selectors.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\napiVersion: \\\"cilium.io/v2\\\"\\nkind: CiliumNetworkPolicy\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"allow-to-kubedns\\\"\\n  namespace: public\\nspec:\\n  endpointSelector:\\n    {}\\n  egress:\\n  - toEndpoints:\\n    - matchLabels:\\n        k8s:io.kubernetes.pod.namespace: kube-system\\n        k8s-app: kube-dns\\n    toPorts:\\n    - ports:\\n      - port: '53'\\n        protocol: UDP\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### CiliumNetworkPolicy for DNS-based Egress\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/security/policy/language\\n\\nThis CiliumNetworkPolicy demonstrates how to allow DNS traffic to a DNS service and enforce egress policy based on a fully qualified domain name (FQDN). It requires the Cilium agent to be configured with L7 proxy enabled and an L7 policy allowing DNS requests. The policy selects pods with the label 'app: test-app' and allows egress to the 'kube-dns' service on port 53 for any DNS pattern, while also allowing connections to 'my-remote-service.com'.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\napiVersion: \\\"cilium.io/v2\\\"\\nkind: CiliumNetworkPolicy\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"to-fqdn\\\"\\nspec:\\n  endpointSelector:\\n    matchLabels:\\n      app: test-app\\n  egress:\\n    - toEndpoints:\\n      - matchLabels:\\n          \\\"k8s:io.kubernetes.pod.namespace\\\": kube-system\\n          \\\"k8s:k8s-app\\\": kube-dns\\n      toPorts:\\n        - ports:\\n           - port: \\\"53\\\"\\n             protocol: ANY\\n          rules:\\n            dns:\\n              - matchPattern: \\\"*\\\"\\n    - toFQDNs:\\n        - matchName: \\\"my-remote-service.com\\\"\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### CiliumNetworkPolicy: Combine DNS, Port, and L7 Rules (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/security/dns\\n\\nThis YAML defines a CiliumNetworkPolicy that restricts egress traffic from 'mediabot' pods. It allows access to '*.github.com' only on port 443 (TCP) and ensures DNS queries to kube-dns on port 53 are permitted. This policy demonstrates combining FQDN, port, and DNS rules.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\napiVersion: \\\"cilium.io/v2\\\"\\nkind: CiliumNetworkPolicy\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"fqdn\\\"\\nspec:\\n  endpointSelector:\\n    matchLabels:\\n      org: empire\\n      class: mediabot\\n  egress:\\n  - toFQDNs:\\n    - matchPattern: \\\"*.github.com\\\"\\n    toPorts:\\n    - ports:\\n      - port: \\\"443\\\"\\n        protocol: TCP\\n  - toEndpoints:\\n    - matchLabels:\\n        \\\"k8s:io.kubernetes.pod.namespace\\\": kube-system\\n        \\\"k8s:k8s-app\\\": kube-dns\\n    toPorts:\\n    - ports:\\n      - port: \\\"53\\\"\\n        protocol: ANY\\n      rules:\\n        dns:\\n        - matchPattern: \\\"*\\\"\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Apply DNS Egress Policy with Exact Name Match (YAML)\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/security/dns\\n\\nThis Cilium Network Policy allows specific pods (mediabot) to access only 'api.github.com' via DNS. It also ensures access to the cluster's DNS service for resolution. This policy uses &#96;matchName&#96; for precise domain matching.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\napiVersion: \\\"cilium.io/v2\\\"\\nkind: CiliumNetworkPolicy\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"fqdn\\\"\\nspec:\\n  endpointSelector:\\n    matchLabels:\\n      org: empire\\n      class: mediabot\\n  egress:\\n  - toFQDNs:\\n    - matchName: \\\"api.github.com\\\"\\n  - toEndpoints:\\n    - matchLabels:\\n        \\\"k8s:io.kubernetes.pod.namespace\\\": kube-system\\n        \\\"k8s:k8s-app\\\": kube-dns\\n    toPorts:\\n    - ports:\\n      - port: \\\"53\\\"\\n        protocol: ANY\\n      rules:\\n        dns:\\n        - matchPattern: \\\"*\\\"\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Default Deny Egress with CiliumNetworkPolicy\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/security/policy/language\\n\\nThis policy places endpoints with the label 'role=restricted' into a default deny mode for egress traffic. An empty egress rule section signifies that no egress traffic is allowed by default. This is useful for enforcing strict outbound network controls.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\napiVersion: \\\"cilium.io/v2\\\"\\nkind: CiliumNetworkPolicy\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"deny-all-egress\\\"\\nspec:\\n  endpointSelector:\\n    matchLabels:\\n      role: restricted\\n  egress:\\n  - {}\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\"}]}"
}
```

```json
{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### Allow Dev Endpoints to Receive Traffic from Cluster Nodes\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/security/policy/language\\n\\nThis policy enables endpoints labeled &#96;env=dev&#96; to receive traffic from any host within the cluster where Cilium is running. The &#96;fromEntities: [host, remote-node]&#96; field specifies that traffic can originate from the host itself or other remote nodes in the cluster.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\napiVersion: \\\"cilium.io/v2\\\"\\nkind: CiliumNetworkPolicy\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"to-dev-from-nodes-in-cluster\\\"\\nspec:\\n  endpointSelector:\\n    matchLabels:\\n      env: dev\\n  ingress:\\n    - fromEntities:\\n      - host\\n      - remote-node\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Allow Local Dev Endpoints to Access Host\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/security/policy/language\\n\\nThis policy allows all endpoints with the label &#96;env=dev&#96; to access the host serving the endpoint. Kubernetes automatically allows communication from local endpoints, but this policy explicitly defines it. The &#96;toEntities: [host]&#96; field specifies the destination.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\napiVersion: \\\"cilium.io/v2\\\"\\nkind: CiliumNetworkPolicy\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"dev-to-host\\\"\\nspec:\\n  endpointSelector:\\n    matchLabels:\\n      env: dev\\n  egress:\\n    - toEntities:\\n      - host\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Example Host Policy for Ingress Workers\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/security/policy/language\\n\\nThis example demonstrates a CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy designed to secure ingress traffic on worker nodes. It uses a node selector to target specific nodes and defines allowed ingress ports and protocols, including essential services like SSH and Kubernetes API.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\napiVersion: \\\"cilium.io/v2\\\"\\nkind: CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy\\nmetadata:\\n  name: \\\"lock-down-ingress-worker-node\\\"\\nspec:\\n  description: \\\"Allow a minimum set of required ports on ingress of worker nodes\\\"\\n  nodeSelector:\\n    matchLabels:\\n      type: ingress-worker\\n  ingress:\\n  - fromEntities:\\n    - remote-node\\n    - health\\n  - toPorts:\\n    - ports:\\n      - port: \\\"22\\\"\\n        protocol: TCP\\n      - port: \\\"6443\\\"\\n        protocol: TCP\\n      - port: \\\"2379\\\"\\n        protocol: TCP\\n      - port: \\\"4240\\\"\\n        protocol: TCP\\n      - port: \\\"8472\\\"\\n        protocol: UDP\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n### Cilium Network Policies &gt; Entities based\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/security/policy/language\\n\\n&#96;fromEntities&#96; is used to describe the entities that can access the selected endpoints. &#96;toEntities&#96; is used to describe the entities that can be accessed by the selected endpoints. The following entities are defined: &#96;host&#96; (includes the local host and containers in host networking mode), &#96;remote-node&#96; (any node other than the local host, including containers in host networking mode), &#96;kube-apiserver&#96; (represents the kube-apiserver within or outside the cluster), &#96;ingress&#96; (represents the Cilium Envoy instance handling ingress L7 traffic, including hairpinning), &#96;cluster&#96; (logical group of all network endpoints inside the local cluster, including managed and unmanaged endpoints, host, remote-node, and init identities, and remote nodes in a clustermesh scenario), &#96;init&#96; (endpoints in bootstrap phase before security identity is resolved, typically in non-Kubernetes environments), &#96;health&#96; (health endpoints for checking cluster connectivity), &#96;unmanaged&#96; (endpoints not managed by Cilium, considered part of the cluster), &#96;world&#96; (all endpoints outside of the cluster, equivalent to CIDR 0.0.0.0/0), and &#96;all&#96; (combination of all known clusters, world, and whitelists all communication).\"}]}"
}
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Planning concise update with risk note**</details></think>

---

Implemented increment 1: **egress choke point + upload size enforcement + demo updates**.

### What I changed

- Added in-cluster upload proxy with body-size cap:
  - `roles/tetragon/files/dlp-egress-proxy.yml`
  - NGINX proxy service in `coder-secure` at `dlp-egress-proxy:8080`
  - `client_max_body_size 50m`
  - `POST /upload` returns `200` if under limit, `413` if over limit

- Added Cilium egress policy for secure workspaces:
  - `roles/tetragon/files/cilium-netpol-egress-via-proxy.yml`
  - For `app.kubernetes.io/name=coder-workspace` in `coder-secure`, allows only:
    - DNS to kube-dns
    - traffic to `dlp-egress-proxy:8080`
    - Coder control-plane connectivity (service + host NodePort path)

- Wired deployment into Ansible:
  - `roles/tetragon/tasks/main.yml`
  - Now copies/applies proxy and `workspace-egress-via-proxy` policy
  - Waits for proxy deployment before applying egress policy

- Updated secure workspace startup message:
  - `templates/podman-workspace-secure/main.tf`

- Updated demo flow:
  - `scripts/demo-dataleakage.sh`
  - Now validates:
    - direct internet blocked
    - `5MB` proxy upload allowed
    - `100MB` proxy upload blocked (`413`)
    - toxic read patterns still killed by Tetragon

### Live validation results

Validated directly in the secure workspace pod (`kubectl exec`):

- Direct `curl https://example.com` -> blocked (timeout) ✅
- Upload `5MB` to proxy `/upload` -> HTTP `200` ✅
- Upload `100MB` to proxy `/upload` -> HTTP `413` ✅

So your requested behavior is now demonstrable: **small push allowed, massive push denied**.

### One note

- `coder list` shows workspace healthy, but `coder ssh` from CLI was intermittently waiting for agent connection under the strict egress profile. The workload controls are functioning, but if you want I can do a second pass to explicitly preserve all agent transport paths while keeping exfil restrictions tight.

If you want, I can proceed with increment 2 next: **hourly cumulative egress alerting** (`200MB/hour` per workspace) using Hubble flow metrics.

---

_**User**_

here nginx egress proxy does not provide ssl bump, could envoy or mitmproxy do that? 

---

_**Agent (model gpt-5.3-codex, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Planning targeted tool comparison**</details></think>

---

Good call — you’re right that plain NGINX proxying here is only doing size-based control, not TLS interception. I’ll quickly verify current Envoy/mitmproxy capabilities from official sources and give you the most practical choice for this lab.

```json
{
```
```json
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"Title: TLS Passthrough | Envoy Gateway\\nAuthor: \\nPublished Date: 2026-02-16T00:00:00.000Z\\nURL: https://gateway.envoyproxy.io/v1.4/tasks/security/tls-passthrough/\\nText: TLS Passthrough | Envoy Gateway\\n[**View page source] [**Edit this page] [**Create child page] [**Create documentation issue] [**Create project issue] [**Print entire section] [**Ask a Question] \\nNotice: You're viewing documentation for an older version of Envoy Gateway that has reached End of Life (EOL).\\nPlease refer to the[/docs] documentation for the most current information.\\n# TLS Passthrough\\n** 3 minute read This task will walk through the steps required to configure TLS Passthrough via Envoy Gateway. Unlike configuring\\nSecure Gateways, where the Gateway terminates the client TLS connection, TLS Passthrough allows the application itself\\nto terminate the TLS connection, while the Gateway routes the requests to the application based on SNI headers.\\n## Prerequisites[] \\n* OpenSSL to generate TLS assets.## Installation[] \\nFollow the steps below to install Envoy Gateway and the example manifest. Before\\nproceeding, you should be able to query the example backend using HTTP.\\nExpand for instructions\\n1. Install the Gateway API CRDs and Envoy Gateway using Helm:\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n&#96;helm install eg oci://docker.io/envoyproxy/gateway-helm --version v1.4.5 -n envoy-gateway-system --create-namespace&#96;\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n2. Install the GatewayClass, Gateway, HTTPRoute and example app:\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n&#96;kubectl apply -f https://github.com/envoyproxy/gateway/releases/download/v1.4.5/quickstart.yaml -n default&#96;\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n3. Verify Connectivity:\\n* With External LoadBalancer Support\\n* Without LoadBalancer Support\\nGet the External IP of the Gateway:\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n&#96;exportGATEWAY\\\\_HOST=$(kubectl get gateway/eg -ojsonpath=&amp;#39;{.status.addresses[0].value}&amp;#39;)&#96;\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nCurl the example app through Envoy proxy:\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n&#96;curl --verbose --header&amp;#34;Host: www.example.com&amp;#34;http://$GATEWAY\\\\_HOST/get&#96;\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nThe above command should succeed with status code 200.\\nGet the name of the Envoy service created the by the example Gateway:\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n&#96;exportENVOY\\\\_SERVICE=$(kubectl get svc -n envoy-gateway-system --selector=gateway.envoyproxy.io/owning-gateway-namespace=default,gateway.envoyproxy.io/owning-gateway-name=eg -ojsonpath=&amp;#39;{.items[0].metadata.name}&amp;#39;)&#96;\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nGet the deployment of the Envoy service created the by the example Gateway:\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n&#96;exportENVOY\\\\_DEPLOYMENT=$(kubectl get deploy -n\\n\\nTitle: TLS Inspector \\nAuthor: \\nURL: https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/listeners/listener_filters/tls_inspector\\nText: TLS Inspector &amp;mdash; envoy 1.35.0-dev-79acec documentation\\n* [] \\n* [Configuration reference] \\n* [Listeners] \\n* [Listener filters] \\n* TLS Inspector\\n* [View page source] \\n# TLS Inspector[] \\nTLS Inspector listener filter allows detecting whether the transport appears to be\\nTLS or plaintext, and if it is TLS, it detects the[Server Name Indication] and/or[Application-Layer Protocol Negotiation] from the client. This can be used to select a[FilterChain] via the[server\\\\_names] and/or[application\\\\_protocols] of a[FilterChainMatch].\\n* [SNI] \\n* This filter should be configured with the type URL&#96;type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.filters.listener.tls\\\\_inspector.v3.TlsInspector&#96;.\\n* [v3 API reference] \\n## Example[] \\nA sample filter configuration could be:\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nlistener\\\\_filters:-name:tls\\\\_inspectortyped\\\\_config:&amp;quot;@type&amp;quot;:type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.filters.listener.tls\\\\_inspector.v3.TlsInspector\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n## Statistics[] \\nThis filter has a statistics tree rooted at*tls\\\\_inspector*with the following statistics:\\n|\\nName\\n|\\nType\\n|\\nDescription\\n|\\nclient\\\\_hello\\\\_too\\\\_large\\n|\\nCounter\\n|\\ntotal unreasonably large client hello received\\n|\\ntls\\\\_found\\n|\\nCounter\\n|\\nTotal number of times TLS was found\\n|\\ntls\\\\_not\\\\_found\\n|\\nCounter\\n|\\nTotal number of times TLS was not found\\n|\\nalpn\\\\_found\\n|\\nCounter\\n|\\nTotal number of times[Application-Layer Protocol Negotiation] was successful\\n|\\nalpn\\\\_not\\\\_found\\n|\\nCounter\\n|\\nTotal number of times[Application-Layer Protocol Negotiation] has failed\\n|\\nsni\\\\_found\\n|\\nCounter\\n|\\nTotal number of times[Server Name Indication] was found\\n|\\nsni\\\\_not\\\\_found\\n|\\nCounter\\n|\\nTotal number of times[Server Name Indication] was not found\\n|\\nbytes\\\\_processed\\n|\\nHistogram\\n|\\nRecords sizes which records the number of bytes the tls\\\\_inspector processed while analyzing for tls usage.\\nIf the connection uses TLS: this is the size of client hello. If the client hello is too large, then the\\nrecorded value will be 64KiB which is the maximum client hello size.\\nIf the connection does not use TLS: it is the number of bytes processed\\nuntil the inspector determined the connection was not using TLS.\\nIf the connection terminates early nothing is recorded if we didn’t have\\nsufficient bytes for either of the cases above.\\n|\\n\\nTitle: TLS Inspector Filter (proto) \\nAuthor: \\nURL: https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/extensions/filters/listener/tls_inspector/v3/tls_inspector.proto\\nText: TLS Inspector Filter (proto) &amp;mdash; envoy 1.35.0-dev-f24509 documentation\\n* [] \\n* [API] \\n* [v3 API reference] \\n* [Extensions] \\n* [Filters] \\n* [Listener filters] \\n* TLS Inspector Filter (proto)\\n* [View page source] \\n# TLS Inspector Filter (proto)[] \\nThis extension has the qualified name&#96;envoy.filters.listener.tls\\\\_inspector&#96;\\nNote\\nThis extension is intended to be robust against both untrusted downstream and\\nupstream traffic.\\nTip\\nThis extension extends and can be used with the following extension category:\\n* [envoy.filters.listener] \\nThis extension must be configured with one of the following type URLs:\\n* [type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.filters.listener.tls\\\\_inspector.v3.TlsInspector] \\nAllows detecting whether the transport appears to be TLS or plaintext.\\n## extensions.filters.listener.tls\\\\_inspector.v3.TlsInspector[] \\n[[extensions.filters.listener.tls\\\\_inspector.v3.TlsInspector proto]] \\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n{&amp;quot;&amp;quot;enable\\\\_ja3\\\\_fingerprinting&amp;quot;&amp;quot;:{...},&amp;quot;&amp;quot;initial\\\\_read\\\\_buffer\\\\_size&amp;quot;&amp;quot;:{...}}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nenable\\\\_ja3\\\\_fingerprinting\\n([BoolValue]) Populate&#96;JA3&#96;fingerprint hash using data from the TLS Client Hello packet. Default is false.\\ninitial\\\\_read\\\\_buffer\\\\_size\\n([UInt32Value]) The size in bytes of the initial buffer requested by the tls\\\\_inspector.\\nIf the filter needs to read additional bytes from the socket, the\\nfilter will double the buffer up to it’s default maximum of 64KiB.\\nIf this size is not defined, defaults to maximum 64KiB that the\\ntls inspector will consume.\\n\\nTitle: TLS inspector listener filter — envoy 1.36.0-dev-9a5db8 documentation\\nAuthor: \\nPublished Date: 2025-01-01T00:00:00.000Z\\nURL: https://envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/start/sandboxes/tls-inspector\\nText: TLS inspector listener filter &amp;mdash; envoy 1.36.0-dev-9a5db8 documentation\\n* [] \\n* [Getting Started] \\n* [Sandboxes] \\n* TLS inspector listener filter\\n* [View page source] \\n# TLS inspector listener filter[] \\nRequirements\\n[Sandbox environment] \\nSetup your sandbox environment with Docker and Docker Compose,\\nand clone the Envoy repository with Git.\\n[curl] \\nUsed to make HTTP requests.\\n[jq] \\nParse JSON output from the upstream echo servers.\\nThis example demonstrates how the TLS inspector can be used to select&#96;FilterChains&#96;to\\ndistribute the traffic between upstream clusters according to the matched&#96;transport\\\\_protocol&#96;and/or&#96;application\\\\_protocols&#96;.\\nIt also demonstrates the admin statistics generated by the TLS inspector listener filter.\\n## Step 1: Build the sandbox[] \\nChange to the&#96;tls-inspector&#96;directory, and bring up the services.\\nThis starts one proxy listening on[http://localhost:10000], and with an admin interface listening on port&#96;12345&#96;.\\nIt also starts three upstream HTTP services that echo back received headers in JSON format.\\nThe first 2 services are HTTPS services listening on port&#96;443&#96;and the other has no TLS and listens on\\nport&#96;80&#96;.\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n$pwdexamples/tls-inspector$dockercomposepull$dockercomposeup--build-d$dockercomposepsName Command State Ports---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------tls-inspector\\\\_service-http\\\\_1 docker-entrypoint.sh node ... Uptls-inspector\\\\_service-https-http1.1\\\\_1 docker-entrypoint.sh node ... Uptls-inspector\\\\_service-https-http2\\\\_1 docker-entrypoint.sh node ... Uptls-inspector\\\\_tls-inspector\\\\_1 /docker-entrypoint.sh /usr ... Up 0.0.0.0:10000-&amp;gt;&amp;gt;10000/tcp, 0.0.0.0:12345-&amp;gt;&amp;gt;12345/tcp\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n## Step 2: Access services[] \\nQuerying the service at port&#96;10000&#96;with a different HTTP version specified over TLS, or\\nwith HTTP protocol without TLS, the requests will be handled by different upstream services.\\n### Query the proxy with HTTP/1.1 and TLS[] \\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n$curl-sk--http1.1https://localhost:10000|jq&amp;#39;.os.hostname&amp;#39;&amp;quot;service-https-http1.1&amp;quot;\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nThe upstream&#96;service-https-http1.1&#96;handles the request.\\n### Query the proxy with HTT\\n\\nTitle: SNI dynamic forward proxy — envoy 1.38.0-dev-d568d5 ...\\nURL: https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/listeners/network_filters/sni_dynamic_forward_proxy_filter\\nText: SNI dynamic forward proxy &amp;mdash; envoy 1.35.0-dev-0d5fd0 documentation\\n* [] \\n* [Configuration reference] \\n* [Listeners] \\n* [Network filters] \\n* SNI dynamic forward proxy\\n* [View page source] \\n# SNI dynamic forward proxy[] \\nAttention\\nSNI dynamic forward proxy support should be considered alpha and not production ready.\\nThrough the combination of[TLS inspector] listener filter,\\nthis network filter and the[dynamic forward proxy cluster],\\nEnvoy supports[Server Name Indication] (SNI) based dynamic forward proxy. The implementation works just like the[HTTP dynamic forward proxy], but using the value in\\nSNI as target host instead.\\nThe following is a complete configuration that configures both this filter\\nas well as the[dynamic forward proxy cluster]. Both filter and cluster\\nmust be configured together and point to the same DNS cache parameters for Envoy to operate as an\\nSNI dynamic forward proxy.\\nNote\\nThe following config doesn’t terminate TLS in listener, so there is no need to configure TLS context\\nin cluster. The TLS handshake is passed through by Envoy.\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nadmin:address:socket\\\\_address:protocol:TCPaddress:127.0.0.1port\\\\_value:9901static\\\\_resources:listeners:-name:listener\\\\_0address:socket\\\\_address:protocol:TCPaddress:0.0.0.0port\\\\_value:10000listener\\\\_filters:-name:envoy.filters.listener.tls\\\\_inspectortyped\\\\_config:&amp;quot;@type&amp;quot;:type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.filters.listener.tls\\\\_inspector.v3.TlsInspectorfilter\\\\_chains:-filters:-name:envoy.filters.network.sni\\\\_dynamic\\\\_forward\\\\_proxytyped\\\\_config:&amp;quot;@type&amp;quot;:type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.filters.network.sni\\\\_dynamic\\\\_forward\\\\_proxy.v3.FilterConfigport\\\\_value:443dns\\\\_cache\\\\_config:name:dynamic\\\\_forward\\\\_proxy\\\\_cache\\\\_configdns\\\\_lookup\\\\_family:V4\\\\_ONLY-name:envoy.tcp\\\\_proxytyped\\\\_config:&amp;quot;@type&amp;quot;:type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.filters.network.tcp\\\\_proxy.v3.TcpProxystat\\\\_prefix:tcpcluster:dynamic\\\\_forward\\\\_proxy\\\\_clusterclusters:-name:dynamic\\\\_forward\\\\_proxy\\\\_clusterlb\\\\_policy:CLUSTER\\\\_PROVIDEDcluster\\\\_type:name:envoy.clusters.dynamic\\\\_forward\\\\_proxytyped\\\\_config:&amp;quot;@type&amp;quot;:type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.clusters.dynamic\\\\_forward\\\\_proxy.v3.ClusterConfigdns\\\\_cache\\\\_config:n\\n\\n\"}]}"
}
```
