```json
{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"Title: Mitmproxy Tutorial: Setup, Usage &amp; Common Fixes (2026) - Seedbox\\nAuthor: Diego Asturias\\nURL: https://www.rapidseedbox.com/blog/mitmproxy\\nText: Mitmproxy: The Complete Handbook. &amp;mdash; RapidSeedbox\\n* [Seedbox] \\n* [Pricing] \\n* [Features] \\n* [What is a Seedbox] \\n* [IP Rentals] \\n* [Proxy] \\n* [Rotating Residental Proxies] \\n* [Datacenter IPv4/IPv6] \\n* [Resources] \\n* [Blog] \\n* [Knowledge Base] \\n* [Help Center] \\n[Log in] Get RapidSeedbox\\nTOP **\\n# Mitmproxy: The Complete Handbook.\\n![Avatar for Diego Asturias] By[Diego Asturias] \\nJune 26, 2024&amp;bull; Last updated on October 16, 2024\\n[#mitmproxy] [#proxy] [#traffic analyzer] [#Web Debugging] \\n*Have you ever felt like you needed a magnifying glass for your network traffic?*\\n*Meet Mitmproxy.*\\nThis open-source powerhouse lets you intercept, modify, and analyze HTTP/1, HTTP/2, and WebSocket traffic. It is perfect for web developers, network admins, and ideal security professionals.\\nThis article covers everything you need to know about Mitmproxy (if you are a beginner).**It provides an overview of the service, its pros and cons, installation steps, basic usage, and advanced features like SSL/TLS setup, Python automation, and Docker integration.**\\n[![mitmproxy]] \\n***Disclaimer:****This material has been developed strictly for informational purposes. It does not constitute endorsement of any activities (including illegal activities), products or services. You are solely responsible for complying with the applicable laws, including intellectual property laws, when using our services or relying on any information herein. We do not accept any liability for damage arising from the use of our services or information contained herein in any manner whatsoever, except where explicitly required by law.*\\n## Table of Contents\\n1. [Overview of Mitmproxy] \\n* What it is.\\n* Pros and cons\\n* Mitmproxy use cases\\n* [How to Install Mitmproxy (Step-by-Step Guide)] \\n* Windows\\n* Linux\\n* macOS\\n* [Getting Started with Mitmproxy] \\n* Launch Mitmproxy\\n* Configure Your Browser or Device\\n* Verify Configuration\\n* [How to Use Mitmproxy] \\n* [More Things You Can Do with Mitmproxy] \\n* Use the Web Interface\\n* Use Mitmdump\\n* Setting Up SSL/TLS Certificates\\n* Automate Tasks with Python\\n* Running Mitmproxy in Docker\\n* [FAQ Section] \\n* [Final Words.] \\n## 1. Overview of Mitmproxy\\nMitmproxy is an open-source powerful toolset for intercepting, modifying, and a\\n\\nTitle: Introduction - mitmproxy Docs\\nURL: https://docs.mitmproxy.org/stable/\\nText: Introduction\\n[![mitmproxy docs]] \\n# [#] Introduction\\nmitmproxy is a set of tools that provide an interactive, SSL/TLS-capable intercepting proxy for HTTP/1, HTTP/2, and WebSockets.\\n## [#] Features\\n* Intercept HTTP &amp;amp; HTTPS requests and responses and modify them on the fly\\n* Save complete HTTP conversations for later replay and analysis\\n* Replay the client-side of an HTTP conversation\\n* Replay HTTP responses of a previously recorded server\\n* Reverse proxy mode to forward traffic to a specified server\\n* Transparent proxy mode on macOS and Linux\\n* Make scripted changes to HTTP traffic using Python\\n* SSL/TLS certificates for interception are generated on the fly\\n* And[much, much more&amp;hellip;] ## [#] 3 Powerful Core Tools\\nThe mitmproxy project&amp;rsquo;s tools are a set of front-ends that expose common\\nunderlying functionality. When we talk about &amp;ldquo;mitmproxy&amp;rdquo; we usually refer to any of the three tools - they\\nare just different front-ends to the same core proxy.\\n**mitmproxy**is an interactive, SSL/TLS-capable intercepting proxy with a console interface for HTTP/1, HTTP/2, and WebSockets.\\n**mitmweb**is a web-based interface for mitmproxy.\\n**mitmdump**is the command-line version of mitmproxy. Think tcpdump for HTTP.\\nDistribution packages can be found on the[mitmproxy website].\\nDevelopment information and our source code can be found in our[GitHub repository].\\n### [#] mitmproxy\\n![] \\n**mitmproxy**is a console tool that allows interactive examination and\\nmodification of HTTP traffic. It differs from mitmdump in that all flows are\\nkept in memory, which means that it&amp;rsquo;s intended for taking and manipulating\\nsmall-ish samples. Use the&#96;?&#96;shortcut key to view, context-sensitive\\ndocumentation from any**mitmproxy**screen.\\n### [#] mitmweb\\n![] \\n**mitmweb**is mitmproxy&amp;rsquo;s web-based user interface that allows\\ninteractive examination and modification of HTTP traffic. Like\\nmitmproxy, it differs from mitmdump in that all flows are kept in\\nmemory, which means that it&amp;rsquo;s intended for taking and manipulating\\nsmall-ish samples.\\nMitmweb is currently in beta. We consider it stable for all features\\ncurrently exposed in the UI, but it still misses a lot of mitmproxy&amp;rsquo;s\\nfeatures.\\n### [#] mitmdump\\n**mitmdump**is the command-line companion to mitmproxy. It provides\\ntcpdump-like fu\\n\\nTitle: Certificates - mitmproxy Docs\\nURL: https://docs.mitmproxy.org/stable/concepts/certificates/\\nText: Certificates[Skip to main content] \\n[Edit on GitHub] # About Certificates[] \\nMitmproxy can decrypt encrypted traffic on the fly, as long as the client trusts\\nmitmproxy&amp;rsquo;s built-in certificate authority. Usually this means that the mitmproxy CA\\ncertificate has to be installed on the client device.\\n## Quick Setup[] \\nBy far the easiest way to install the mitmproxy CA certificate is to use the\\nbuilt-in certificate installation app. To do this, start mitmproxy and\\nconfigure your target device with the correct proxy settings. Now start a\\nbrowser on the device, and visit the magic domain[mitm.it]. You should see\\nsomething like this:\\n![] \\nClick on the relevant icon, follow the setup instructions for the platform\\nyou&amp;rsquo;re on and you are good to go.\\n## The mitmproxy certificate authority[] \\nThe first time mitmproxy is run, it creates the keys for a certificate\\nauthority (CA) in the config directory (&#96;\\\\~/.mitmproxy&#96;by default).\\nThis CA is used for on-the-fly generation of dummy certificates for each visited website.\\nSince your browser won&amp;rsquo;t trust the mitmproxy CA out of the box, you will either need to click through a TLS certificate\\nwarning on every domain, or install the CA certificate once so that it is trusted.\\nThe following files are created:\\n|Filename|Contents|\\nmitmproxy-ca.pem|The certificate**and the private key**in PEM format.|\\nmitmproxy-ca-cert.pem|The certificate in PEM format. Use this to distribute on most non-Windows platforms.|\\nmitmproxy-ca-cert.p12|The certificate in PKCS12 format. For use on Windows.|\\nmitmproxy-ca-cert.cer|Same file as .pem, but with an extension expected by some Android devices.|\\nFor security reasons, the mitmproxy CA is generated uniquely on the first start and\\nis not shared between mitmproxy installations on different devices. This makes sure\\nthat other mitmproxy users cannot intercept your traffic.\\n### Installing the mitmproxy CA certificate manually[] \\nSometimes using the[quick install app] is not an option and you need to install the CA manually.\\nBelow is a list of pointers to manual certificate installation\\ndocumentation for some common platforms. The mitmproxy CA cert is located in&#96;\\\\~/.mitmproxy&#96;after it has been generated at the first start of mitmproxy.\\n* curl on the command line:\\n&#96;curl --proxy 127.0.0.1:8\\n\\nTitle: Traffic analysis with mitmproxy - DigitalOcean\\nURL: https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/traffic-analysis-with-mitmproxy\\nText: Traffic analysis with mitmproxy | DigitalOcean\\n[DigitalOcean\\n] \\n* Log in\\n* Log in to:\\n* [Community] \\n* [DigitalOcean] \\n* Sign up\\n* Sign up for:\\n* [Community] \\n* [DigitalOcean] \\n## Report this\\nWhat is the reason for this report?\\nThis undefined is spam\\nThis undefined is offensive\\nThis undefined is off-topic\\nThis undefined is other\\nSubmit\\n[Tutorial] \\n# Traffic analysis with mitmproxy\\nPublished on September 29, 2025\\n[] [] [] [] \\n[Linux] \\n[Bash] \\n[Ubuntu] \\n![Holger Reibold] \\nBy[Holger Reibold] \\n![Traffic analysis with mitmproxy] \\nTable of contents\\nPopular topics\\nCompanies that provide web services for the outside world in their own infrastructure are exposed to a variety of threats. The developers of the[open source Mitmproxy] tool describe it as the Swiss army knife for debugging, testing, data protection analysis, and penetration testing HTTP(S) connections. I show you how mitmproxy can be a useful addition to your security toolbox.\\n## [Key Takeaways] [] \\n* **Man-in-the-Middle Capability**:[Mitmproxy] acts as an intermediary between clients and servers, intercepting and decrypting both HTTP and HTTPS traffic for analysis and manipulation\\n* **Certificate Management**: The tool generates its own[CA certificates] on-the-fly and provides easy installation methods across different platforms (Windows, Linux, macOS, mobile devices)\\n* **Multiple Interface Options**: Offers three interfaces - command-line (&#96;mitmproxy&#96;), web GUI (&#96;mitmweb&#96;), and scriptable (&#96;mitmdump&#96;) - catering to different user preferences and automation needs\\n* **Advanced Traffic Manipulation**: Supports real-time request/response editing, traffic replay, filtering, and selective interception using regex patterns and flow filters\\n* **Extensible Architecture**: Features a modular design with Python scripting support and a library of community-developed extensions for custom functionality\\n* **Cross-Platform Support**: Available for Linux, macOS, Windows, and mobile platforms with Docker containerization options for easy deployment\\n* **Security Testing Focus**: Specifically designed for penetration testing, debugging, data protection analysis, and web application security assessment## [Man in the Middle] [] \\nWhen most people hear the term &#96;&#96;proxy,‘’ they pro\\n\\nTitle: How mitmproxy works\\nURL: https://docs.mitmproxy.org/stable/concepts/how-mitmproxy-works/\\nText: How mitmproxy works Skip to main content\\n\\n[Edit on GitHub] \\n\\n# How mitmproxy works\\n\\nMitmproxy is an enormously flexible tool. Knowing exactly how the proxying process works will help you deploy it creatively, and take into account its fundamental assumptions and how to work around them. This document explains mitmproxy’s proxy mechanism in detail, starting with the simplest unencrypted explicit proxying, and working up to the most complicated interaction -transparent proxying of TLS-protected traffic1 in the presence of [Server Name Indication].\\n\\n## Explicit HTTP\\n\\nConfiguring the client to use mitmproxy as an explicit proxy is the simplest and most reliable way to intercept traffic. The proxy protocol is codified in the [HTTP RFC], so the behaviour of both the client and the server is well defined, and usually reliable. In the simplest possible interaction with mitmproxy, a client connects directly to the proxy, and makes a request that looks like this:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nGET http://example.com/index.html HTTP/1.1\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\nThis is a proxy GET request - an extended form of the vanilla HTTP GET request that includes a schema and host specification, and it includes all the information mitmproxy needs to proceed.\\n\\n#### Explicit\\n\\n1. Mitmproxy connects to the upstream server and simply forwards the request on.\\n2. The client connects to the proxy and makes a request.\\n\\n## Explicit HTTPS\\n\\nThe process for an explicitly proxied HTTPS connection is quite different. The client connects to the proxy and makes a request that looks like this:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nCONNECT example.com:443 HTTP/1.1\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\nA conventional proxy can neither view nor manipulate a TLS-encrypted data stream, so a CONNECT request simply asks the proxy to open a pipe between the client and server. The proxy here is just a facilitator - it blindly forwards data in both directions without knowing anything about the contents. The negotiation of the TLS connection happens over this pipe, and the subsequent flow of requests and responses are completely opaque to the proxy.\\n\\n### The MITM in mitmproxy\\n\\nThis is where mitmproxy’s fundamental trick comes into play. The MITM in its name stands for Man-In-The-Middle - a reference to the process we use to intercept and interfere with these theoretically opaque data streams. The basic idea is to \\n\\n\"}]}"
```
```json
}
```

```json
{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"Title: TLS Passthrough | Envoy Gateway\\nAuthor: \\nPublished Date: 2026-02-24T00:00:00.000Z\\nURL: https://gateway.envoyproxy.io/v1.5/tasks/security/tls-passthrough/\\nText: TLS Passthrough | Envoy Gateway\\n\\n# TLS Passthrough\\n\\nThis task will walk through the steps required to configure TLS Passthrough via Envoy Gateway. Unlike configuring Secure Gateways, where the Gateway terminates the client TLS connection, TLS Passthrough allows the application itself to terminate the TLS connection, while the Gateway routes the requests to the application based on SNI headers.\\n\\n## Prerequisites\\n\\n- OpenSSL to generate TLS assets.\\n\\n## Installation\\n\\nFollow the steps below to install Envoy Gateway and the example manifest. Before proceeding, you should be able to query the example backend using HTTP.\\n\\nExpand for instructions\\n\\nInstall the Gateway API CRDs and Envoy Gateway using Helm:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nhelm install eg oci://docker.io/envoyproxy/gateway-helm --version v1.5.9 -n envoy-gateway-system --create-namespace\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\nInstall the GatewayClass, Gateway, HTTPRoute and example app:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nkubectl apply -f https://github.com/envoyproxy/gateway/releases/download/v1.5.9/quickstart.yaml -n default\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\nVerify Connectivity:\\n\\n- Without LoadBalancer Support\\n- With External LoadBalancer Support\\n\\nGet the External IP of the Gateway:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nexport GATEWAY_HOST=$(kubectl get gateway/eg -o jsonpath='{.status.addresses[0].value}')\\n   \\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\nCurl the example app through Envoy proxy:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\ncurl --verbose --header \\\"Host: www.example.com\\\" http://$GATEWAY_HOST/get\\n   \\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\nThe above command should succeed with status code 200.\\n\\nGet the name of the Envoy service created the by the example Gateway:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nexport ENVOY_SERVICE=$(kubectl get svc -n envoy-gateway-system --selector=gateway.envoyproxy.io/owning-gateway-namespace=default,gateway.envoyproxy.io/owning-gateway-name=eg -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')\\n   \\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\nGet the deployment of the Envoy service created the by the example Gateway:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\nexport ENVOY_DEPLOYMENT\\n\\nTitle: TLS Termination for TCP - Envoy Gateway\\nURL: https://gateway.envoyproxy.io/docs/tasks/security/tls-termination/\\nText: TLS Termination for TCP | Envoy Gateway\\n[**View page source] [**Edit this page] [**Create child page] [**Create documentation issue] [**Create project issue] [**Print entire section] \\n# TLS Termination for TCP\\n** 3 minute read This task will walk through the steps required to configure TLS Terminate mode for TCP traffic via Envoy Gateway.\\nThis task uses a self-signed CA, so it should be used for testing and demonstration purposes only.\\n## Prerequisites[] \\n* OpenSSL to generate TLS assets.## Installation[] \\nFollow the steps below to install Envoy Gateway and the example manifest. Before\\nproceeding, you should be able to query the example backend using HTTP.\\nExpand for instructions\\n1. Install the Gateway API CRDs and Envoy Gateway using Helm:\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n&#96;helm install eg oci://docker.io/envoyproxy/gateway-helm --version v1.3.0 -n envoy-gateway-system --create-namespace&#96;\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n2. Install the GatewayClass, Gateway, HTTPRoute and example app:\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n&#96;kubectl apply -f https://github.com/envoyproxy/gateway/releases/download/v1.3.0/quickstart.yaml -n default&#96;\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n3. Verify Connectivity:\\n* With External LoadBalancer Support\\n* Without LoadBalancer Support\\nYou can also test the same functionality by sending traffic to the External IP. To get the external IP of the\\nEnvoy service, run:\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n&#96;exportGATEWAY\\\\_HOST=$(kubectl get gateway/eg -ojsonpath=&amp;#39;{.status.addresses[0].value}&amp;#39;)&#96;\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n**Note**: In certain environments, the load balancer may be exposed using a hostname, instead of an IP address. If so, replace&#96;ip&#96;in the above command with&#96;hostname&#96;.\\nCurl the example app through Envoy proxy:\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n&#96;curl --verbose --header&amp;#34;Host: https://projectcontour.io/docs/1.29/config/tls-termination/\\nText: # Documentation\\n\\n1.29\\n\\n[main] [1.33] [1.32] [1.31] [1.30] [1.29] [1.28] [1.27] [1.26] [1.25] [1.24] [1.23] [1.22] [1.21] Termination\\nURL: https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-01-27-envoy-tls-termination/view\\nText: How to Configure Envoy TLS Termination\\n\\n# How to Configure Envoy TLS Termination\\n\\nA comprehensive guide to configuring TLS termination in Envoy Proxy, covering certificate management, SDS-based rotation, mTLS authentication, and ALPN protocol negotiation for secure service communication.\\n\\n[@nawazdhandala] Jan 27, 2026\\n\\n[Envoy] [TLS] [Security] [mTLS] [HTTPS] [Proxy] [Load Balancer] [DevOps] [Kubernetes] \\n\\n---\\n\\n\\\"Security is not a product, but a process.\\\" - Bruce Schneier\\n\\nTLS termination is the process of decrypting encrypted traffic at a proxy before forwarding it to backend services. Envoy Proxy excels at this, offering flexible certificate management, automatic rotation via SDS, and mutual TLS for zero-trust architectures.\\n\\n## Understanding TLS Termination in Envoy\\n\\nWhen Envoy terminates TLS, it handles the cryptographic handshake with clients, decrypts the traffic, and forwards plaintext (or re-encrypted) requests to upstream services.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;mermaid\\nsequenceDiagram\\n    participant Client\\n    participant Envoy\\n    participant Backend\\n\\n    Client-Envoy: TLS Handshake (ClientHello)\\n    Envoy-Client: ServerHello + Certificate\\n    Client-Envoy: Key Exchange\\n    Client-Envoy: Encrypted Request\\n    Note over Envoy: Decrypt TLS\\n    Envoy-Backend: Plaintext Request\\n    Backend-Envoy: Plaintext Response\\n    Note over Envoy: Encrypt TLS\\n    Envoy-Client: Encrypted Response\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n### Benefits of TLS Termination at the Edge\\n\\n- Easier debugging - Inspect plaintext traffic between Envoy and backends\\n- Better performance - Offload CPU-intensive crypto from application servers\\n- Reduced backend complexity - Services don't need TLS configuration\\n- Centralized certificate management - Update certificates in one place\\n\\n## Basic TLS Configuration\\n\\nStart with a simple listener that terminates TLS using static certificates.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\\n# envoy.yaml - Basic TLS term\\n\\nTitle: Transport Layer Security (TLS) \\nAuthor: \\nURL: https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/start/sandboxes/tls\\nText: Transport Layer Security (TLS) — envoy 1.38.0-dev-d568d5 documentation\\n\\n# Transport Layer Security (TLS)\\n\\nThis example walks through some of the ways that Envoy can be configured to make use of encrypted connections using HTTP over TLS.\\n\\nIt demonstrates a number of commonly used proxying and TLS termination patterns:\\n\\n&#96;https&#96;-&gt;&#96;http&#96;\\n\\n&#96;https&#96;-&gt;&#96;https&#96;\\n\\n&#96;http&#96;-&gt;&#96;https&#96;\\n\\n&#96;https&#96; passthrough\\n\\nTo better understand the provided examples, and for a description of how TLS is configured with Envoy, please see the [securing Envoy quick start guide].\\n\\nWarning\\n\\nFor the sake of simplicity, the examples provided here do not authenticate any client certificates, or validate any of the provided certificates.\\n\\nWhen using TLS, you are strongly encouraged to [validate] all certificates wherever possible.\\n\\nYou should also [authenticate clients] where you control both sides of the connection, or relevant protocols are available.\\n\\n## Step 1: Build the sandbox\\n\\nChange to the&#96;tls&#96; directory.\\n\\nThis starts four proxies listening on&#96;localhost&#96; ports&#96;10000-10003&#96;.\\n\\nIt also starts two upstream services, one HTTP and one HTTPS, which echo back received headers in JSON format.\\n\\nThe upstream services listen on the internal Docker network on ports&#96;80&#96; and&#96;443&#96; respectively.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n$ pwd\\nexamples/tls\\n$ docker compose pull\\n$ docker compose up --build -d\\n$ docker compose ps\\n\\n       Name                            Command                 State          Ports\\n-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\\ntls_proxy-https-to-http_1       /docker-entrypoint.sh /usr ... Up      0.0.0.0:10000-&gt;10000/tcp\\ntls_proxy-https-to-https_1      /docker-entrypoint.sh /usr ... Up      0.0.0.0:10001-&gt;10000/tcp\\ntls_proxy-http-to-https_1       /docker-entrypoint.sh /usr ... Up      0.0.0.0:10002-&gt;10000/tcp\\ntls_proxy-https-passthrough_1   /docker-ent\\n\\nTitle: Client TLS authentication \\nAuthor: \\nURL: https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/listeners/network_filters/client_ssl_auth_filter\\nText: Client TLS authentication — envoy 1.38.0-dev-d568d5 documentation\\n\\n# Client TLS authentication\\n\\nClient TLS authentication filter [architecture overview] \\n\\nThis filter should be configured with the type URL&#96;type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.filters.network.client_ssl_auth.v3.ClientSSLAuth&#96;.\\n\\n[v3 API reference] \\n\\n## Statistics\\n\\nEvery configured client TLS authentication filter has statistics rooted at auth.clientssl.. with the following statistics:\\n\\nName\\n\\nType\\n\\nDescription\\n\\nupdate_success\\n\\nCounter\\n\\nTotal principal update successes\\n\\nupdate_failure\\n\\nCounter\\n\\nTotal principal update failures\\n\\nauth_no_ssl\\n\\nCounter\\n\\nTotal connections ignored due to no TLS\\n\\nauth_ip_allowlist\\n\\nCounter\\n\\nTotal connections allowed due to the IP allowlist\\n\\nauth_digest_match\\n\\nCounter\\n\\nTotal connections allowed due to certificate match\\n\\nauth_digest_no_match\\n\\nCounter\\n\\nTotal connections denied due to no certificate match\\n\\ntotal_principals\\n\\nGauge\\n\\nTotal loaded principals\\n\\n## REST API\\n\\nGET /v1/certs/list/approved\\n\\nThe authentication filter will call this API every refresh interval to fetch the current list of approved certificates/principals. The expected JSON response looks like:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n{\\n  \\\"certificates\\\": []\\n}\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\ncertificates\\n\\n(required, array) list of approved certificates/principals.\\n\\nEach certificate object is defined as:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n{\\n  \\\"fingerprint_sha256\\\": \\\"...\\\",\\n}\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\nfingerprint_sha256\\n\\n(required, string) The SHA256 hash of the approved client certificate. Envoy will match this hash to the presented client certificate to determine whether there is a digest match.\\n\\n\"}]}"
}
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Clarifying Envoy TLS interception limits**</details></think>

---

Yes — **mitmproxy can do true SSL bump/TLS interception**.  
Envoy can do parts of it, but for your use case it’s usually more complex.

- **mitmproxy:** built for interception/MITM of HTTPS, generates per-site certs on the fly, requires clients to trust its CA.  
- **Envoy:** great as egress proxy, TLS termination, passthrough, SNI-based forwarding — but “generic outbound SSL bump for arbitrary CONNECT traffic” is not the simple/default path. It’s more of a custom architecture project.
- **Practical takeaway for this lab:** if you want payload-aware DLP quickly, use `mitmproxy` (or similar explicit MITM proxy) behind your Cilium egress policy.

### Important caveat
Even with SSL bump, some traffic still escapes full inspection:
- certificate pinning apps,
- non-HTTP encrypted protocols,
- clients not configured to trust your interception CA.

### Suggested approach
- Keep current proxy for coarse controls (size limits, routing).
- Add a dedicated MITM proxy for selected workloads/domains only.
- Force egress from `coder-secure` through that proxy via Cilium policy.
- Start in monitor mode before hard-blocking.

Sources:
- https://docs.mitmproxy.org/stable/
- https://docs.mitmproxy.org/stable/concepts/certificates/
- https://docs.mitmproxy.org/stable/concepts/how-mitmproxy-works/
- https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/listeners/network_filters/sni_dynamic_forward_proxy_filter
- https://gateway.envoyproxy.io/docs/tasks/security/tls-termination/
- https://gateway.envoyproxy.io/v1.5/tasks/security/tls-passthrough/

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: POD=$(kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l k8s-app=cilium -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}'); kubectl -n kube-system exec "$POD" -c cilium-agent -- cilium-dbg metrics list | rg -i "byte|hubble|flow|drop|verdict|dns|egress"</summary>

```bash
POD=$(kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l k8s-app=cilium -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}'); kubectl -n kube-system exec "$POD" -c cilium-agent -- cilium-dbg metrics list | rg -i "byte|hubble|flow|drop|verdict|dns|egress"
```

```
Command 'rg' not found, but can be installed with:
sudo snap install ripgrep  # version 12.1.0, or
sudo apt  install ripgrep  # version 14.0.3-1
See 'snap info ripgrep' for additional versions.

```
</details>
</tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: POD=$(kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l k8s-app=cilium -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}'); kubectl -n kube-system exec "$POD" -c cilium-agent -- cilium-dbg metrics list</summary>

```bash
POD=$(kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l k8s-app=cilium -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}'); kubectl -n kube-system exec "$POD" -c cilium-agent -- cilium-dbg metrics list
```

```json
      "parameters": "{\"libraryId\":\"/websites/cilium_io_en_stable\",\"query\":\"hubble observe command output json fields verdict source destination bytes l4 since 1h\"}",
```

Result:

```json
{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### Observe Policy Verdicts with Hubble CLI\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/security/policy-creation\\n\\nThis command uses the Hubble CLI to observe network flow policy verdicts. It retrieves the last policy verdict, showing whether traffic was allowed, denied, or audited.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\\n$ kubectl -n \\\"$CILIUM_NAMESPACE\\\" exec \\\"$CILIUM_POD\\\" -c cilium-agent -- \\\\\\n    hubble observe flows -t policy-verdict --last 1\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\\n$ kubectl -n \\\"$CILIUM_NAMESPACE\\\" exec \\\"$CILIUM_POD\\\" -c cilium-agent -- \\\\\\n    hubble observe flows -t policy-verdict -o json --last 1\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Example Hubble Exported Event Output (JSON)\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/observability/hubble/configuration/export\\n\\nShows an example of the JSON output generated by Hubble Exporter. This output reflects the structure defined by the &#96;fieldMask&#96; configuration, including details like timestamps, verdicts, IP addresses, ports, node names, and drop reasons. This format is useful for parsing and analyzing network flow data.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;json\\n{\\\"flow\\\":{\\\"time\\\":\\\"2023-08-21T12:12:13.517394084Z\\\",\\\"verdict\\\":\\\"DROPPED\\\",\\\"IP\\\":{\\\"source\\\":\\\"fe80::64d8:8aff:fe72:fc14\\\",\\\"destination\\\":\\\"ff02::2\\\",\\\"ipVersion\\\":\\\"IPv6\\\"},\\\"l4\\\":{\\\"ICMPv6\\\":{\\\"type\\\":133}},\\\"source\\\":{},\\\"destination\\\":{},\\\"node_name\\\":\\\"kind-kind/kind-worker\\\",\\\"drop_reason_desc\\\":\\\"INVALID_SOURCE_IP\\\"},\\\"node_name\\\":\\\"kind-kind/kind-worker\\\",\\\"time\\\":\\\"2023-08-21T12:12:13.517394084Z\\\"}\\n{\\\"flow\\\":{\\\"time\\\":\\\"2023-08-21T12:12:18.510175415Z\\\",\\\"verdict\\\":\\\"DROPPED\\\",\\\"IP\\\":{\\\"source\\\":\\\"10.244.1.60\\\",\\\"destination\\\":\\\"10.244.1.5\\\",\\\"ipVersion\\\":\\\"IPv4\\\"},\\\"l4\\\":{\\\"TCP\\\":{\\\"source_port\\\":44916,\\\"destination_port\\\":80,\\\"flags\\\":{\\\"SYN\\\":true}}},\\\"source\\\":{\\\"namespace\\\":\\\"default\\\",\\\"pod_name\\\":\\\"xwing\\\"},\\\"destination\\\":{\\\"namespace\\\":\\\"default\\\",\\\"pod_name\\\":\\\"deathstar-7848d6c4d5-th9v2\\\"},\\\"node_name\\\":\\\"kind-kind/kind-worker\\\",\\\"drop_reason_desc\\\":\\\"POLICY_DENIED\\\"},\\\"node_name\\\":\\\"kind-kind/kind-worker\\\",\\\"time\\\":\\\"2023-08-21T12:12:18.510175415Z\\\"}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Configure Hubble Export Field Mask (Shell)\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/observability/hubble/configuration/export\\n\\nDemonstrates how to configure the Hubble export field mask using shell commands. This allows users to specify which fields from the flow proto definition should be included in the exported data. It's useful for tailoring the exported logs to specific needs, such as excluding certain information or focusing on critical details.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;shell\\nhubble-export-fieldmask: time source.identity source.namespace source.pod_name destination.identity destination.namespace destination.pod_name source_service destination_service l4 IP ethernet l7 Type node_name is_reply event_type verdict Summary\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;shell\\nhubble-export-fieldmask: time source.namespace source.pod_name destination.namespace destination.pod_name l4 IP node_name is_reply verdict\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Observe Network Flows for a Specific Pod with hubble observe\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/operations/troubleshooting\\n\\nThis command, executed via &#96;kubectl exec&#96;, allows observation of network flow events related to a specific pod within the last specified duration. It requires the Cilium agent to be running with Hubble enabled and the pod to be managed by Cilium.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\\n$ kubectl exec -n kube-system cilium-77lk6 -- hubble observe --since 3m --pod default/tiefighter\\nMay  4 12:47:08.811: default/tiefighter:53875 -&gt; kube-system/coredns-74ff55c5b-66f4n:53 to-endpoint FORWARDED (UDP)\\nMay  4 12:47:08.811: default/tiefighter:53875 -&gt; kube-system/coredns-74ff55c5b-66f4n:53 to-endpoint FORWARDED (UDP)\\nMay  4 12:47:08.811: default/tiefighter:53875 &lt;- kube-system/coredns-74ff55c5b-66f4n:53 to-endpoint FORWARDED (UDP)\\nMay  4 12:47:08.811: default/tiefighter:53875 &lt;- kube-system/coredns-74ff55c5b-66f4n:53 to-endpoint FORWARDED (UDP)\\nMay  4 12:47:08.811: default/tiefighter:50214 &lt;&gt; default/deathstar-c74d84667-cx5kp:80 to-overlay FORWARDED (TCP Flags: SYN)\\nMay  4 12:47:08.812: default/tiefighter:50214 &lt;- default/deathstar-c74d84667-cx5kp:80 to-endpoint FORWARDED (TCP Flags: SYN, ACK)\\nMay  4 12:47:08.812: default/tiefighter:50214 &lt;&gt; default/deathstar-c74d84667-cx5kp:80 to-overlay FORWARDED (TCP Flags: ACK)\\nMay  4 12:47:08.812: default/tiefighter:50214 &lt;&gt; default/deathstar-c74d84667-cx5kp:80 to-overlay FORWARDED (TCP Flags: ACK, PSH)\\nMay  4 12:47:08.812: default/tiefighter:50214 &lt;- default/deathstar-c74d84667-cx5kp:80 to-endpoint FORWARDED (TCP Flags: ACK, PSH)\\nMay  4 12:47:08.812: default/tiefighter:50214 &lt;&gt; default/deathstar-c74d84667-cx5kp:80 to-overlay FORWARDED (TCP Flags: ACK, FIN)\\nMay  4 12:47:08.812: default/tiefighter:50214 &lt;- default/deathstar-c74d84667-cx5kp:80 to-endpoint FORWARDED (TCP Flags: ACK, FIN)\\nMay  4 12:47:08.812: default/tiefighter:50214 &lt;&gt; default/deathstar-c74d84667-cx5kp:80 to-overlay FORWARDED (TCP Flags: ACK)\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Observer Service RPC Endpoints\\n\\nSource: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/internals/hubble\\n\\nThe Observer service is the principal gRPC service provided by the Hubble server. It offers endpoints for retrieving network flows, node information, namespace data, and server status.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;APIDOC\\n## Observer Service RPC Endpoints\\n\\n### Description\\nThe Observer service provides four main RPC endpoints: &#96;GetFlows&#96;, &#96;GetNodes&#96;, &#96;GetNamespaces&#96;, and &#96;ServerStatus&#96;. It also includes &#96;GetAgentEvents&#96; and &#96;GetDebugEvents&#96; for accessing Cilium agent and datapath debug events.\\n\\n### Method\\nAll endpoints are gRPC RPC calls.\\n\\n### Endpoints\\n- &#96;GetFlows&#96;\\n- &#96;GetNodes&#96;\\n- &#96;GetNamespaces&#96;\\n- &#96;ServerStatus&#96;\\n- &#96;GetAgentEvents&#96;\\n- &#96;GetDebugEvents&#96;\\n\\n### Parameters\\n#### GetFlows Parameters\\n- **allowlist** (list of filters) - Optional - Filters to include specific flows.\\n- **denylist** (list of filters) - Optional - Filters to exclude specific flows.\\n\\n*Note: When both allowlist and denylist are provided, flows matched by the allowlist and not by the denylist are returned.*\\n\\n#### GetNodes Parameters\\nNone\\n\\n#### GetNamespaces Parameters\\nNone\\n\\n#### ServerStatus Parameters\\nNone\\n\\n#### GetAgentEvents Parameters\\nNone\\n\\n#### GetDebugEvents Parameters\\nNone\\n\\n### Request Example\\n*Note: gRPC requests are typically made using gRPC client libraries and do not have a simple JSON request body format like REST APIs.*\\n\\n### Response\\n#### Success Response (200)\\n- **GetFlows**: Stream of flow related events.\\n- **GetNodes**: List of metrics and information related to each Hubble instance.\\n- **GetNamespaces**: List of namespaces with network flows in the last hour.\\n- **ServerStatus**: Summary of information from &#96;GetNodes&#96;.\\n- **GetAgentEvents**: Stream of Cilium agent events.\\n- **GetDebugEvents**: Stream of Cilium datapath debug events.\\n\\n#### Response Example\\n*Note: gRPC responses are streamed or returned as specific protobuf messages.*\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;protobuf\\n// Example for GetFlows response (simplified)\\nmessage Flow { \\n  // ... flow details ...\\n}\\n\\n// Example for GetNodes response (simplified)\\nmessage NodeInfo {\\n  string name = 1;\\n  // ... other node metrics ...\\n}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\"}]}"
}
```

</tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: POD=$(kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l k8s-app=cilium -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}'); kubectl -n kube-system exec "$POD" -c cilium-agent -- hubble observe --help</summary>

```bash
POD=$(kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l k8s-app=cilium -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}'); kubectl -n kube-system exec "$POD" -c cilium-agent -- hubble observe --help
```

```
Observe flows and events of a Hubble server

Usage:
  hubble observe [flags]
  hubble observe [command]

Available Commands:
  agent-events Observe Cilium agent events
  debug-events Observe Cilium debug events
  flows        Observe flows of a Hubble server

Selectors Flags:
      --all            Get all flows stored in Hubble's buffer. Note: this option may cause Hubble to return a lot of data. It is recommended to only use it along filters to limit the amount of data returned.
      --first uint     Get first N flows stored in Hubble's buffer. When querying against Hubble Relay, this gets N flows per instance of Hubble connected to that Relay.
  -f, --follow         Follow flows output
      --last uint      Get last N flows stored in Hubble's buffer (default 20). When querying against Hubble Relay, this gets N flows per instance of Hubble connected to that Relay.
      --since string   Filter flows since a specific date. The format is relative (e.g. 3s, 4m, 1h43,, ...) or one of:
                         StampMilli:             Jan _2 15:04:05.000
                         YearMonthDay:           2006-01-02
                         YearMonthDayHour:       2006-01-02T15-07:00
                         YearMonthDayHourMinute: 2006-01-02T15:04-07:00
                         RFC3339:                2006-01-02T15:04:05-07:00
                         RFC3339Milli:           2006-01-02T15:04:05.999-07:00
                         RFC3339Micro:           2006-01-02T15:04:05.999999-07:00
                         RFC3339Nano:            2006-01-02T15:04:05.999999999-07:00
                         RFC1123Z:               Mon, 02 Jan 2006 15:04:05 -0700
                        
      --until string   Filter flows until a specific date. The format is relative (e.g. 3s, 4m, 1h43,, ...) or one of:
                         StampMilli:             Jan _2 15:04:05.000
                         YearMonthDay:           2006-01-02
                         YearMonthDayHour:       2006-01-02T15-07:00
                         YearMonthDayHourMinute: 2006-01-02T15:04-07:00
                         RFC3339:                2006-01-02T15:04:05-07:00
                         RFC3339Milli:           2006-01-02T15:04:05.999-07:00
                         RFC3339Micro:           2006-01-02T15:04:05.999999-07:00
                         RFC3339Nano:            2006-01-02T15:04:05.999999999-07:00
                         RFC1123Z:               Mon, 02 Jan 2006 15:04:05 -0700
                        

Filters Flags:
  -A, --all-namespaces filter[=true]        Show all flows in any Kubernetes namespace.
      --cel-expression filter               Filter flows using the given CEL expression
      --cluster filter                      Show all flows which match the cluster names (e.g. "test-cluster", "prod-*")
      --drop-reason-desc filter             Show only flows which match this drop reason describe (e.g. "POLICY_DENIED", "UNSUPPORTED_L3_PROTOCOL")
      --fqdn filter                         Show all flows related to the given fully qualified domain name (e.g. "*.cilium.io").
      --from-all-namespaces filter[=true]   Show flows originating in any Kubernetes namespace.
      --from-cluster filter                 Show all flows originating from endpoints known to be in the given cluster name
      --from-fqdn filter                    Show all flows originating at the given fully qualified domain name (e.g. "*.cilium.io").
      --from-identity filter                Show all flows originating at an endpoint with the given security identity
      --from-ip filter                      Show all flows originating at the given IP address. Each of the source IPs can be specified as an exact match (e.g. '1.1.1.1') or as a CIDR range (e.g.'1.1.1.0/24').
      --from-label filter                   Show only flows originating in an endpoint with the given labels (e.g. "key1=value1", "reserved:world")
      --from-namespace filter               Show all flows originating in the given Kubernetes namespace.
      --from-pod filter                     Show all flows originating in the given pod name prefix([namespace/]&lt;pod-name&gt;). If namespace is not provided, 'default' is used
      --from-port filter                    Show only flows with the given source port (e.g. 8080)
      --from-service filter                 Shows flows where the source IP address matches the ClusterIP address of the given service name prefix([namespace/]&lt;svc-name&gt;). If namespace is not provided, 'default' is used
      --from-workload filter                Show all flows originating at an endpoint with the given workload
      --http-header filter                  Show only flows which match this HTTP header key:value pairs (e.g. "foo:bar")
      --http-method filter                  Show only flows which match this HTTP method (e.g. "get", "post")
      --http-path filter                    Show only flows which match this HTTP path regular expressions (e.g. "/page/\\d+")
      --http-status filter                  Show only flows which match this HTTP status code prefix (e.g. "404", "5+")
      --http-url filter                     Show only flows which match this HTTP URL regular expressions (e.g. "http://.*cilium\.io/page/\\d+")
      --identity filter                     Show all flows related to an endpoint with the given security identity
      --interface filter                    Show all flows observed at the given interface name (e.g. eth0)
      --ip filter                           Show all flows originating or terminating at the given IP address. Each of the IPs can be specified as an exact match (e.g. '1.1.1.1') or as a CIDR range (e.g.'1.1.1.0/24').
      --ip-version filter                   Show only IPv4, IPv6 flows or non IP flows (e.g. ARP packets) (ie: "none", "v4", "v6")
  -4, --ipv4 filter[=v4]                    Show only IPv4 flows
  -6, --ipv6 filter[=v6]                    Show only IPv6 flows
  -l, --label filter                        Show only flows related to an endpoint with the given labels (e.g. "key1=value1", "reserved:world")
  -n, --namespace filter                    Show all flows related to the given Kubernetes namespace.
      --node-label filter                   Show only flows observed on nodes matching the given label filter (e.g. "key1=value1", "io.cilium/egress-gateway")
      --node-name filter                    Show all flows which match the given node names (e.g. "k8s*", "test-cluster/*.company.com")
      --not filter[=true]                   Reverses the next filter to be blacklist i.e. --not --from-ip 2.2.2.2
      --pod filter                          Show all flows related to the given pod name prefix ([namespace/]&lt;pod-name&gt;). If namespace is not provided, 'default' is used.
      --port filter                         Show only flows with given port in either source or destination (e.g. 8080)
      --protocol filter                     Show only flows which match the given L4/L7 flow protocol (e.g. "udp", "http")
      --service filter                      Shows flows where either the source or destination IP address matches the ClusterIP address of the given service name prefix ([namespace/]&lt;svc-name&gt;). If namespace is not provided, 'default' is used. 
      --snat-ip filter                      Show all flows SNATed with the given IP address. Each of the SNAT IPs can be specified as an exact match (e.g. '1.1.1.1') or as a CIDR range (e.g.'1.1.1.0/24').
      --tcp-flags filter                    Show only flows which match the given TCP flags (e.g. "syn", "ack", "fin")
      --to-all-namespaces filter[=true]     Show flows terminating in any Kubernetes namespace.
      --to-cluster filter                   Show all flows destined to endpoints known to be in the given cluster name
      --to-fqdn filter                      Show all flows terminating at the given fully qualified domain name (e.g. "*.cilium.io").
      --to-identity filter                  Show all flows terminating at an endpoint with the given security identity
      --to-ip filter                        Show all flows terminating at the given IP address. Each of the destination IPs can be specified as an exact match (e.g. '1.1.1.1') or as a CIDR range (e.g.'1.1.1.0/24').
      --to-label filter                     Show only flows terminating in an endpoint with given labels (e.g. "key1=value1", "reserved:world")
      --to-namespace filter                 Show all flows terminating in the given Kubernetes namespace.
      --to-pod filter                       Show all flows terminating in the given pod name prefix([namespace/]&lt;pod-name&gt;). If namespace is not provided, 'default' is used
      --to-port filter                      Show only flows with the given destination port (e.g. 8080)
      --to-service filter                   Shows flows where the destination IP address matches the ClusterIP address of the given service name prefix ([namespace/]&lt;svc-name&gt;). If namespace is not provided, 'default' is used
      --to-workload filter                  Show all flows terminating at an endpoint with the given workload
      --trace-id filter                     Show only flows which match this trace ID
      --traffic-direction filter            Show all flows in the given traffic direction (either ingress or egress)
  -t, --type filter                         Filter by event types TYPE[:SUBTYPE]. Available types and subtypes:
                                            TYPE             SUBTYPE
                                            capture          n/a
                                            drop             n/a
                                            l7               n/a
                                            policy-verdict   n/a
                                            trace            from-endpoint
                                                             from-host
                                                             from-network
                                                             from-overlay
                                                             from-proxy
                                                             from-stack
                                                             to-endpoint
                                                             to-host
                                                             to-network
                                                             to-overlay
                                                             to-proxy
                                                             to-stack
                                            trace-sock       n/a
      --uuid filter                         Show the only flow matching this unique flow identifier, if any
      --verdict filter                      Show only flows with this verdict [FORWARDED, DROPPED, AUDIT, REDIRECTED, ERROR, TRACED, TRANSLATED]
      --workload filter                     Show all flows related to an endpoint with the given workload

Raw-Filters Flags:
      --allowlist stringArray   Specify allowlist as JSON encoded FlowFilters
      --denylist stringArray    Specify denylist as JSON encoded FlowFilters

Formatting Flags:
  -o, --output string        Specify the output format, one of:
                               compact:  Compact output
                               dict:     Each flow is shown as KEY:VALUE pair
                               jsonpb:   JSON encoded GetFlowResponse according to proto3's JSON mapping
                               json:     Alias for jsonpb
                               table:    Tab-aligned columns
                              (default "compact")
      --print-node-name      Print node name in output
      --time-format string   Specify the time format for printing. This option does not apply to the json and jsonpb output type. One of:
                               StampMilli:             Jan _2 15:04:05.000
                               YearMonthDay:           2006-01-02
                               YearMonthDayHour:       2006-01-02T15Z07:00
                               YearMonthDayHourMinute: 2006-01-02T15:04Z07:00
                               RFC3339:                2006-01-02T15:04:05Z07:00
                               RFC3339Milli:           2006-01-02T15:04:05.999Z07:00
                               RFC3339Micro:           2006-01-02T15:04:05.999999Z07:00
                               RFC3339Nano:            2006-01-02T15:04:05.999999999Z07:00
                               RFC1123Z:               Mon, 02 Jan 2006 15:04:05 -0700
                               (default "StampMilli")

Flow Format Flags:
      --color string     Colorize the output when the output format is one of 'compact' or 'dict'. The value is one of 'auto' (default), 'always' or 'never' (default "auto")
      --ip-translation   Translate IP addresses to logical names such as pod name, FQDN, ... (default true)
      --numeric          Display all information in numeric form

Server Flags:
      --basic-auth-password string    Specify a password for basic auth
      --basic-auth-username string    Specify a username for basic auth
      --kube-context string           Kubernetes configuration context. This option is only considered when --port-forward is set.
      --kube-namespace string         Namespace Cilium is running in. This option is only considered when --port-forward is set. (default "kube-system")
      --kubeconfig string             Path to the kubeconfig file. This option is only considered when --port-forward is set.
  -P, --port-forward                  Automatically forward the relay port to the local machine. Analoguous to running: 'cilium hubble port-forward'.
      --port-forward-port uint16      Local port to forward to. 0 will select a random port. This option is only considered when --port-forward is set. (default 4245)
      --request-timeout duration      Unary Request timeout. Only applies to non-streaming RPCs (ServerStatus, ListNodes, ListNamespaces). (default 12s)
      --server string                 Address of a Hubble server. Ignored when --input-file or --port-forward is provided. (default "localhost:4245")
      --timeout duration              Hubble server dialing timeout (default 5s)
      --tls                           Specify that TLS must be used when establishing a connection to a Hubble server.
                                      By default, TLS is only enabled if the server address starts with 'tls://'.
      --tls-allow-insecure            Allows the client to skip verifying the server's certificate chain and host name.
                                      This option is NOT recommended as, in this mode, TLS is susceptible to machine-in-the-middle attacks.
                                      See also the 'tls-server-name' option which allows setting the server name.
      --tls-ca-cert-files strings     Paths to custom Certificate Authority (CA) certificate files.The files must contain PEM encoded data.
      --tls-client-cert-file string   Path to the public key file for the client certificate to connect to a Hubble server (implies TLS).
                                      The file must contain PEM encoded data.
      --tls-client-key-file string    Path to the private key file for the client certificate to connect a Hubble server (implies TLS).
                                      The file must contain PEM encoded data.
      --tls-server-name string        Specify a server name to verify the hostname on the returned certificate (eg: 'instance.hubble-relay.cilium.io').

Other Flags:
      --experimental-field-mask strings        Experimental: Comma-separated list of fields for mask. Fields not in the mask will be removed from server response.
      --experimental-use-default-field-masks   Experimental: request only visible fields when the output format is compact, tab, or dict.
      --input-file string                      Query flows from this file instead of the server. Use '-' to read from stdin.
      --print-raw-filters                      Print allowlist/denylist filters and exit without sending the request to Hubble server
  -s, --silent-errors                          Silently ignores errors and warnings

Global Flags:
      --config string   Optional config file (default "/root/.config/hubble/config.yaml")
  -D, --debug           Enable debug messages

Get help:
  -h, --help	Help for any command or subcommand

Use "hubble observe [command] --help" for more information about a command.

```
</details>
</tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: POD=$(kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l k8s-app=cilium -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}'); kubectl -n kube-system exec "$POD" -c cilium-agent -- hubble observe --since 5m --namespace coder-secure --last 5 -o json</summary>

```bash
POD=$(kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l k8s-app=cilium -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}'); kubectl -n kube-system exec "$POD" -c cilium-agent -- hubble observe --since 5m --namespace coder-secure --last 5 -o json
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Designing egress byte tracking via proxy logs**</details></think>

---

I found a better way for increment 2 in this architecture: since all egress is forced through your proxy, we can do accurate per-workspace volume alerting from proxy request sizes (last 1h), without depending on missing Hubble byte fields. I’m implementing that now.


I’ve deployed the logging update and generated sample upload traffic. Next I’m running the new hourly egress alert command to verify it reports per-workspace bytes correctly.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Summarizing increment2 completion**</details></think>

---

Implemented increment 2: **hourly cumulative egress alerting per workspace** (with `200MB/hour` default), using the enforced proxy path.

### What I added

- Proxy log format now includes upload size metadata (`content_length`):
  - `roles/tetragon/files/dlp-egress-proxy.yml`
- Tetragon role now restarts proxy when proxy config changes:
  - `roles/tetragon/tasks/main.yml`
- New alert script:
  - `scripts/alert-egress-volume.py`
  - Reads `dlp-egress-proxy` logs for a time window (default `1h`)
  - Aggregates bytes per source pod IP
  - Maps IP -> workspace pod in `coder-secure`
  - Flags entries over threshold; exits `2` on alert
- New `mise` task:
  - `mise.toml` -> `tasks.egress-alert`
  - Runs: `uv run python scripts/alert-egress-volume.py --namespace coder-secure --window 1h --threshold-mb 200`

### Validation I ran

- Generated a `6MB` upload from `coder-secure` workspace via proxy.
- Script with low threshold (`5MB`) correctly triggered alert:
  - workspace pod `coder-jeremiebelaid-dlp-simple` reported `6.00MB` -> `ALERT`.
- Default task (`200MB`) reports `OK` as expected:
  - `mise run egress-alert`

### Notes

- This alerting is accurate for **HTTP uploads going through the proxy**, since it uses logged `Content-Length`.
- It complements your existing behavior-based Tetragon controls and the egress-via-proxy policy already in place.

---

I confirmed the root issue: workspace can reach `dlp-egress-proxy`, but cannot reach Coder (`coder` service / NodePort), so the agent bootstrap never downloads. I’m now checking live Hubble drops to pinpoint which policy leg is denying that path, then I’ll patch the rule accordingly.

---

Fixed — the workspace agent was blocked by policy on the Coder backend port.

### Root cause

Hubble showed repeated drops from your workspace pod to Coder pod on **TCP 8080**:

- `coder-secure/coder-... -> coder/coder-...:8080 EGRESS DENIED`

Your policy only allowed Coder on port `80`, but service/backend traffic ended up on `8080`.

### Change made

Updated `roles/tetragon/files/cilium-netpol-egress-via-proxy.yml` to allow both:

- `80/TCP`
- `8080/TCP`

### Verified

From workspace pod:

- `http://coder.coder.svc.cluster.local:80/bin/coder-linux-amd64` ✅
- `http://172.26.122.221:30080/bin/coder-linux-amd64` ✅

From WSL CLI:

- `coder ssh dlp-simple 'sh -lc "echo agent_ok"'` ✅ returned `agent_ok`

You should now be able to connect normally from your terminal again.

---

_**User**_

@\home\jeremie\.cursor\projects\home-jeremie-projects-infra\terminals\3.txt:64 ok now it connects but i am logging as root, I need to login as jeremiebelaid my actual user

Got it — I’ll adjust the secure workspace template so shell sessions open as your actual workspace user (`jeremiebelaid`) instead of `root`, then verify with `coder ssh`.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Switching from command to args in templates**</details></think>

---

I found the cause: the workspace template overrides the image entrypoint, so the user-switch logic never runs and you land as `root`. I’m patching the secure template to pass the init script as container args (keeping entrypoint active), then I’ll repush/restart and verify `whoami`.

```
coder v2.30.2+fa050ee

USAGE:
  coder update [flags] &lt;workspace&gt;

  Will update and start a given workspace if it is out of date. If the workspace
  is already running, it will be stopped first.

  Use --always-prompt to change the parameter values of the workspace.

OPTIONS:
      --always-prompt bool
          Always prompt all parameters. Does not pull parameter values from
          existing workspace.

      --build-option string-array, $CODER_BUILD_OPTION
          Build option value in the format "name=value".
          DEPRECATED: Use --ephemeral-parameter instead.

      --build-options bool
          Prompt for one-time build options defined with ephemeral parameters.
          DEPRECATED: Use --prompt-ephemeral-parameters instead.

      --ephemeral-parameter string-array, $CODER_EPHEMERAL_PARAMETER
          Set the value of ephemeral parameters defined in the template. The
          format is "name=value".

      --parameter string-array, $CODER_RICH_PARAMETER
          Rich parameter value in the format "name=value".

      --parameter-default string-array, $CODER_RICH_PARAMETER_DEFAULT
          Rich parameter default values in the format "name=value".

      --prompt-ephemeral-parameters bool, $CODER_PROMPT_EPHEMERAL_PARAMETERS
          Prompt to set values of ephemeral parameters defined in the template.
          If a value has been set via --ephemeral-parameter, it will not be
          prompted for.

      --rich-parameter-file string, $CODER_RICH_PARAMETER_FILE
          Specify a file path with values for rich parameters defined in the
          template. The file should be in YAML format, containing key-value
          pairs for the parameters.

———
Run &#96;coder --help&#96; for a list of global options.
```

```
NAME                             READY   STATUS   RESTARTS      AGE   IP            NODE    NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES
coder-jeremiebelaid-dlp-simple   0/1     Error    4 (56s ago)   99s   10.42.0.245   eva02   &lt;none&gt;           &lt;none&gt;
Name:             coder-jeremiebelaid-dlp-simple
Namespace:        coder-secure
Priority:         0
Service Account:  default
Node:             eva02/172.26.122.221
Start Time:       Sat, 28 Feb 2026 13:39:41 +0100
Labels:           app.kubernetes.io/name=coder-workspace
                  app.kubernetes.io/part-of=coder
                  coder.workspace.id=5e06d598-dab9-47ad-a796-6fa0f940f5f6
                  coder.workspace.owner=jeremiebelaid
Annotations:      &lt;none&gt;
Status:           Running
IP:               10.42.0.245
IPs:
  IP:  10.42.0.245
Containers:
  dev:
    Container ID:  containerd://454242d471964b4711570ef37eef2219f415d82842bee7e83d37fb9e98ca6aa2
    Image:         coder-podman:latest
    Image ID:      sha256:4f6b99043e360e2115c30691f581474e099a67d6fc7192a640f844d4fc2170e1
    Port:          &lt;none&gt;
    Host Port:     &lt;none&gt;
    Args:
      sh
      -c
      #!/usr/bin/env sh
      set -eux
      # Sleep for a good long while before exiting.
      # This is to allow folks to exec into a failed workspace and poke around to
      # troubleshoot.
      waitonexit() {
        echo "=== Agent script exited with non-zero code ($?). Sleeping 24h to preserve logs..."
        sleep 86400
      }
      trap waitonexit EXIT
      BINARY_DIR="${BINARY_DIR:-$(mktemp -d -t coder.XXXXXX)}"
      BINARY_NAME=coder
      BINARY_URL=http://172.26.122.221:30080/bin/coder-linux-amd64
      cd "$BINARY_DIR"
      # Attempt to download the coder agent.
      # This could fail for a number of reasons, many of which are likely transient.
      # So just keep trying!
      while :; do
        # Try a number of different download tools, as we don not know what we
        # will have available.
        status=""
        if command -v curl &gt;/dev/null 2&gt;&amp;1; then
          curl -fsSL --compressed "${BINARY_URL}" -o "${BINARY_NAME}" &amp;&amp; break
          status=$?
        elif command -v wget &gt;/dev/null 2&gt;&amp;1; then
          wget -q "${BINARY_URL}" -O "${BINARY_NAME}" &amp;&amp; break
          status=$?
        elif command -v busybox &gt;/dev/null 2&gt;&amp;1; then
          busybox wget -q "${BINARY_URL}" -O "${BINARY_NAME}" &amp;&amp; break
          status=$?
        else
          echo "error: no download tool found, please install curl, wget or busybox wget"
          exit 127
        fi
        echo "error: failed to download coder agent"
        echo "       command returned: ${status}"
        echo "Trying again in 30 seconds..."
        sleep 30
      done
      
      if ! chmod +x $BINARY_NAME; then
        echo "Failed to make $BINARY_NAME executable"
        exit 1
      fi
      
      haslibcap2() {
        command -v setcap /dev/null 2&gt;&amp;1
        command -v capsh /dev/null 2&gt;&amp;1
      }
      printnetadminmissing() {
        echo "The root user does not have CAP_NET_ADMIN permission. " + \
          "If running in Docker, add the capability to the container for " + \
          "improved network performance."
        echo "This has security implications. See https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/capabilities.7.html"
      }
      
      # Attempt to add CAP_NET_ADMIN to the agent binary. This allows us to increase
      # network buffers which improves network transfer speeds.
      if [ -n "${USE_CAP_NET_ADMIN:-}" ]; then
        # If running as root, we do not need to do anything.
        if [ "$(id -u)" -eq 0 ]; then
          echo "Running as root, skipping setcap"
          # Warn the user if root does not have CAP_NET_ADMIN.
          if ! capsh --has-p=CAP_NET_ADMIN; then
            printnetadminmissing
          fi
      
        # If not running as root, make sure we have sudo perms and the "setcap" +
        # "capsh" binaries exist.
        elif sudo -nl &amp;&amp; haslibcap2; then
          # Make sure the root user has CAP_NET_ADMIN.
          if sudo -n capsh --has-p=CAP_NET_ADMIN; then
            sudo -n setcap CAP_NET_ADMIN=+ep ./$BINARY_NAME || true
          else
            printnetadminmissing
          fi
      
        # If we are not running as root, cant sudo, and "setcap" does not exist, we
        # cannot do anything.
        else
          echo "Unable to setcap agent binary. To enable improved network performance, " + \
            "give the agent passwordless sudo permissions and the \"setcap\" + \"capsh\" binaries."
          echo "This has security implications. See https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/capabilities.7.html"
        fi
      fi
      
      export CODER_AGENT_AUTH="token"
      export CODER_AGENT_URL="http://172.26.122.221:30080/"
      
      output=$(./${BINARY_NAME} --version | head -n1)
      if ! echo "${output}" | grep -q Coder; then
        echo &gt;&amp;2 "ERROR: Downloaded agent binary returned unexpected version output"
        echo &gt;&amp;2 "${BINARY_NAME} --version output: \"${output}\""
        exit 2
      fi
      
      exec ./${BINARY_NAME} agent
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Planning entrypoint patch for user switch**</details></think>

---

I traced the crash: after enabling entrypoint-based user switch, the pod now crashes in that switch path (`runuser -l ...`) before the agent starts. I’m applying a minimal fix in the image entrypoint (`runuser -u`), rebuilding the workspace image, and restarting `dlp-simple` to verify you land as `jeremiebelaid`.
